subtopics.name: Main idea / central theme
subtopics.summary: Tests whether you can name the single controlling idea the whole passage is built to defend, phrased as 'the central theme', 'the most appropriate title', or 'the primary purpose of the passage'. XAT passages are more abstract, philosophical and literary than CAT's, so the thesis is often stated obliquely and must be paraphrased rather than lifted from one line.
keyIdeas: Separate the main idea (the claim the whole passage argues for) from the mere topic (what it is about) and from a true-but-narrow supporting detail.
On dense XAT passages, paraphrase each sentence into plain language first; the central theme is what survives when you compress the whole thing to one sentence.
Find the pivot — often a 'but / however / yet' following a commonly held view — the correct answer echoes the author's turn, not the view being challenged.
Reject options that are too broad (extend past the passage's scope) or too narrow (cover only one paragraph or example).

example.q:
Passage: We mistake memory for a faithful recording device, when in truth each act of remembering rewrites the very event it recalls. The past we carry is therefore less an archive than a manuscript endlessly revised by the present self; what we call 'looking back' is really a kind of authorship. Q: Which of the following best captures the central theme of the passage?
(A) Memory reliably preserves the past as a fixed archive of events.
(B) Remembering is a reconstructive act in which the present self continually rewrites the remembered past.
(C) People forget far more of their past than they are able to retain.
(D) The self has no genuine continuity from one moment to the next.
(E) Written manuscripts are more trustworthy records than human memory.
example.solution: The controlling claim is that memory reconstructs rather than records — each recollection revises the event, making remembering an act of 'authorship'. (B) states exactly this. (A) restates the belief the author sets up to reject; (C) and (D) are unsupported; (E) inverts the manuscript metaphor, which describes memory itself, not paper records. Answer: (B).

traps: Choosing the conventional view the author introduces precisely in order to overturn it.
Picking a striking detail or single example instead of the idea the whole passage defends.
Selecting an option that claims more than the abstract passage actually establishes.

subtopics.name: Inference & implication
subtopics.summary: Asks for a conclusion that must be true given the passage but is not stated word-for-word, phrased as 'which of the following can be inferred', 'the passage implies', or 'the author would most likely agree that'. It is the single most frequently tested RC type on XAT, and the exam's abstract, argumentative passages make the required logical step subtle.
keyIdeas: A valid inference is a small step the text guarantees, not new information; if you must import an outside assumption, the option is wrong.
Distrust extreme quantifiers (all / never / only / must) unless the passage is itself that categorical — XAT inference answers are usually hedged.
For 'the author would agree' items, reason from the author's demonstrated stance and the direction of the argument, not from what is merely reasonable in the world.
When two options survive, prefer the weaker, better-qualified one; it is harder to falsify against the text.

example.q:
Passage: That a painting hangs in a museum tells us it was once thought worth preserving; it tells us nothing about whether those who pass it today feel anything at all. Reverence, unlike a canvas, cannot be inherited — it must be renewed in each spectator, or it is not reverence but habit. Q: Which of the following can most reasonably be inferred from the passage?
(A) Museums ought to stop preserving paintings from the past.
(B) The paintings currently in museums have lost all artistic value.
(C) An artwork's preserved status alone cannot guarantee that viewers today feel genuine reverence.
(D) Visitors today feel nothing whatsoever when they view museum paintings.
(E) Habit is, in every case, preferable to reverence.
example.solution: The author says reverence must be renewed in each spectator and that preservation records only past valuation, not present feeling. The guaranteed step is that preserved status by itself cannot secure genuine reverence — (C). (D) overstates: the passage says preservation 'tells us nothing' about present feeling, not that viewers feel nothing. (A), (B) and (E) are unsupported. Answer: (C).

traps: Choosing an option that is reasonable in the real world but not actually licensed by the passage.
Picking an overstated inference (a categorical claim) when the text only warrants a hedged one.
Mistaking a near-verbatim restatement of an explicit line for the intended inference, which requires a distinct logical step.

subtopics.name: Author's tone, attitude & purpose
subtopics.summary: Asks how the author feels about the subject or why a passage or paragraph was written, phrased as 'the tone of the passage is', 'the author's attitude is best described as', or 'the primary purpose of the author is to'. XAT's literary and philosophical passages carry a discernible evaluative slant, often ironic, and the options are calibrated by degree so intensity matters as much as direction.
keyIdeas: Read the author's own evaluative words — loaded adjectives, irony, understatement — they reveal approval, skepticism or contempt more reliably than the topic does.
Calibrate intensity: XAT distinguishes 'critical' from 'contemptuous' and 'appreciative' from 'laudatory'; pick the degree the text supports, not the strongest word.
Separate the author's tone from the tone of a view the author is merely reporting or quoting; do not attribute an opponent's hostility to the author.
For purpose questions, choose the right verb (to argue, to satirize, to lament, to reconcile, to trace) that matches what the passage actually does.

example.q:
Passage: Our age has invented the smart everything — smart phones, smart homes, smart cities — and in the same breath quietly retired the older adjective, wise. We are, it seems, delighted to be endlessly clever about our means and altogether incurious about our ends. Q: The author's tone in the passage is best described as:
(A) Enthusiastic and celebratory
(B) Wryly critical
(C) Neutral and purely descriptive
(D) Bitterly despairing
(E) Nostalgic and sentimental
example.solution: The ironic contrast — 'smart everything' against the quietly 'retired' word wise, and being 'delighted... altogether incurious about our ends' — signals disapproval delivered through irony. That is measured mockery, not celebration, neutrality, or despair. 'Wryly critical' fits the degree exactly: (B). (A) reverses it; (C) misses the slant; (D) overstates intensity; (E) names the wrong flavour. Answer: (B).

traps: Over-reading intensity — labelling ironic criticism as 'contempt' or mild disapproval as 'hatred'.
Attributing the tone of a quoted or opposing viewpoint to the author.
Choosing 'neutral/objective' for a passage that in fact carries a clear evaluative or ironic edge.

subtopics.name: Specific detail / fact-based
subtopics.summary: Asks what the passage explicitly states, phrased as 'according to the passage', 'the author cites X as an example of', or 'which of the following is stated'. The answer is on the page, but XAT hides it by paraphrasing the correct option and surrounding it with distractors that swap details, reverse a claim, or exaggerate a qualifier.
keyIdeas: Return to the exact lines that mention the question's keyword rather than answering from a general impression of the passage.
The correct option is usually a paraphrase, not a copy — match meaning, not vocabulary.
Check each option's scope and qualifiers: distractors keep the passage's words but change 'some' to 'all', flip cause and effect, or assign a view to the wrong party.
Confirm the option is stated, not merely plausible or implied; detail questions demand explicit textual support.

example.q:
Passage: Diderot observed that a single new dressing gown had made him discontent with his shabby old desk, then his chairs, then his prints, until his whole study had been renewed to match one garment. He blamed not his own vanity but the tyranny of the coordinated whole, which demands that each object answer to the rest. Q: According to the passage, Diderot attributed his cascade of purchases to:
(A) His personal vanity
(B) A sudden shortage of money
(C) The pressure of a coordinated whole into which one new object had to be matched
(D) The poor craftsmanship of his old desk
(E) The advice of fashionable friends
example.solution: The passage states he 'blamed not his own vanity but the tyranny of the coordinated whole, which demands that each object answer to the rest'. (C) paraphrases this precisely. (A) is the explanation the text explicitly denies; (B), (D) and (E) are never mentioned. Answer: (C).

traps: Answering from memory of the passage instead of relocating the specific line.
Falling for an option that repeats the passage's exact words but alters a qualifier or reverses the claim (here, 'vanity').
Selecting a detail that is true and present in the passage but does not answer the precise thing asked.

subtopics.name: Vocabulary-in-context
subtopics.summary: Asks what a specific word or phrase means as it is used in the passage, phrased as 'the word X most nearly means' or 'by Y the author refers to'. XAT keeps vocabulary live (CAT dropped standalone vocab), and here the sense is fixed by context, often a secondary or figurative meaning rather than the word's most common dictionary sense.
keyIdeas: Cover the target word, read the sentence, and predict a substitute from the surrounding logic before looking at the options.
Choose the sense the context demands even when the word has a more familiar everyday meaning — XAT often tests a secondary or figurative sense.
Respect connotation: pick a synonym whose positive or negative colour matches the author's stance in that sentence.
Plug your chosen option back into the sentence to confirm it preserves the exact intended meaning.

example.q:
Passage: His apology was a masterpiece of the oblique: he regretted only that offence had been taken, named no deed of his own, and left the room with his conscience as unruffled as when he had entered. Q: As used in the passage, the word 'oblique' most nearly means:
(A) Steeply sloping
(B) Indirect and evasive
(C) Sincere and heartfelt
(D) Angry and confrontational
(E) Brief and hurried
example.solution: The surrounding detail defines the word in place: he regrets only that 'offence had been taken', admits no deed, and leaves with an 'unruffled' conscience — an apology that dodges all responsibility, i.e. indirect and evasive. (B). (A) is the literal geometric sense; (C) reverses the meaning; (D) and (E) do not fit the context. Answer: (B).

traps: Picking the word's most common dictionary meaning when the context calls for a secondary or figurative sense.
Ignoring the negative colouring the sentence gives the word and choosing a neutral or positive synonym.
Selecting a synonym that fits the word in isolation but not the specific sentence it appears in.

subtopics.name: Logical structure & function
subtopics.summary: Asks how the passage is organized or what role a part plays, phrased as 'the passage proceeds by', 'the primary function of the second paragraph is', or 'the author refers to X in order to'. XAT tests whether you can see the architecture of an argument — which sentence is a claim, which a concession, which an example planted to unsettle a definition.
keyIdeas: Build a one-line map of each paragraph's job (claim, counter-view, evidence, concession, resolution) as you read, so 'function' questions become lookups.
Use signpost words — 'however', 'for instance', 'consider', 'granted', 'in contrast' — to track shifts between assertion, illustration, objection and conclusion.
For 'the author refers to X in order to' items, ask what the sentence does for the argument (support, illustrate, qualify, rebut), not what it literally says.
Match the whole answer: a structure option is wrong if even one clause mislabels a step (e.g. calls an example a rebuttal).

example.q:
Passage: Freedom, we are told, means having options. But consider a man in a burning building who may leave by any of five doors: he has five options and no freedom worth the name, for every door opens onto the same fire. The example is meant to unsettle a definition, not to describe a fire. Q: The primary function of the burning-building example is to:
(A) Warn readers about the importance of fire safety
(B) Prove that every building should have at least five exits
(C) Challenge the equation of freedom with the mere availability of options
(D) Show that having more options is always better than having fewer
(E) Recount a real event the author personally witnessed
example.solution: The passage signposts the answer itself: the example is 'meant to unsettle a definition, not to describe a fire.' It is a counter-case showing that many options can coexist with no meaningful freedom, thereby undercutting the definition 'freedom = options'. (C). (A) and (E) read it literally; (B) and (D) invert its point. Answer: (C).

traps: Choosing an option whose overall shape is right but which mislabels one component (calling an illustration a counterargument).
Confusing what a sentence literally says with the function it serves in the argument.
Reading an example planted to challenge a claim as though it endorsed or merely decorated that claim.

subtopics.name: Poetry & verse passages
subtopics.summary: A stimulus type unique to XAT among the major exams — CAT dropped it, XAT keeps it live at roughly one to two questions. A short poem or stanza is set as the passage and you are asked for its theme, tone, the meaning of a central image or metaphor, or what a line implies. The answer turns on figurative reading, not literal paraphrase.
keyIdeas: Identify the central image or metaphor and decide what it stands for; the whole poem usually pivots on that one figure.
Watch for the 'turn' (volta) — the line where the sentiment or argument shifts — the correct answer often lives after it.
Infer tone from diction, rhythm and contrast, not from the literal subject; a poem about a lighthouse is rarely about lighthouses.
Never read verse literally: reject options that fasten on a surface detail the poem subordinates to its metaphor.

example.q:
Poem: 'The lighthouse does not travel to the ship, / Nor call across the water, nor beseech; / It only stands, and keeps its burning grip / On one fixed point — and that is how it teaches.' Q: The lighthouse in the poem is best understood as an image of:
(A) The futility of ever trying to help others
(B) Guidance offered through steadfast constancy rather than active pursuit
(C) The loneliness and isolation of coastal life
(D) The physical danger the open sea poses to ships
(E) The superiority of machines over human effort
example.solution: The poem sets what the lighthouse does not do (travel, call, beseech) against what it does — stand fixed and burn — and names this 'how it teaches'. The controlling metaphor is influence through steadfast constancy rather than active intervention: (B). (A) misreads it (the lighthouse does help, by standing firm); (C), (D) and (E) seize on literal surface details the poem subordinates to its metaphor. Answer: (B).

traps: Reading the poem literally and picking an option about the surface subject (the sea, the coast, machinery).
Missing the contrast the poem is built on — here, passivity that nonetheless guides — and inverting the message.
Choosing an extreme reading (total futility, total superiority) that the measured imagery does not support.

subtopics.name: Cartoon / image-based questions
subtopics.summary: Another XAT-only stimulus among the major exams — CAT never uses it. A political or editorial cartoon (or a described image) is presented and you are asked what it satirizes, the cartoonist's stance, or the message carried by a symbol or caption. It appears at roughly one to two questions and rewards decoding of symbols, exaggeration and irony rather than literal description.
keyIdeas: Identify what each figure or symbol represents — labels, national emblems, a caricatured official or industry — before interpreting the whole.
Read exaggeration and distortion as the target of the satire; whatever is drawn absurdly large or absurdly proud is usually what is being mocked.
Let the caption sharpen or invert the image: irony lives in the gap between what the picture shows and what the words claim.
State the cartoonist's implied judgment in one line; the correct option names that judgment, not a neutral description of the drawing.

example.q:
Cartoon (described): A towering factory belches thick black smoke that, at the very top, has been shaped into a lush green tree. A tiny beaming official on the ground points up at the smoke-tree while holding out a certificate labelled 'Green Award'. The caption below reads: 'Sustainability achieved.' Q: The cartoon is best interpreted as a satire of:
(A) The genuine technical difficulty of reducing factory emissions
(B) Greenwashing — claiming environmental virtue while the underlying harm continues
(C) The natural beauty that trees bring to industrial areas
(D) The impressive speed of governments in granting awards
(E) The falling profitability of heavy manufacturing
example.solution: The visual irony carries the message: real pollution (black smoke) is cosmetically shaped into a tree and then celebrated with a 'Green Award' and the caption 'Sustainability achieved.' The gap between environmental image and unchanged reality is exactly greenwashing: (B). (A), (D) and (E) ignore the irony; (C) reads the smoke-tree literally instead of as satire. Answer: (B).

traps: Describing the image literally instead of naming the judgment the cartoonist is making.
Missing the caption-versus-image irony that carries the whole point.
Picking a real-world issue that is merely adjacent to the drawing (emissions technology, profitability) rather than the target the exaggeration actually mocks.

subtopics.name: Para-jumbles
subtopics.summary: You are given 4-6 scrambled sentences and must restore the original paragraph. Unlike CAT (which now asks these as type-in-the-answer with no options), XAT keeps para-jumbles as a normal 5-option MCQ, so you can back-solve from the choices and negative marking (-0.25) makes eliminating impossible orders very efficient.
keyIdeas: Find the opening sentence first: it introduces a person/thing by full name, sets time or place, and needs no earlier context.
Chase mandatory pairs using links -- pronouns (it, this, such, they), connectors (however, therefore, moreover) and definite references ('such figures') must point back to something already named.
Use the options as a filter: if two sentences must be adjacent, delete every option that splits them; often one or two orders survive.
The concluding sentence usually generalises, evaluates, or contrasts ('However, the deeper significance...') -- it caps the paragraph, so park it last.

example.q:
Arrange sentences A-D to form a coherent paragraph.
A. However, the deeper significance of the discovery lay not in the object itself but in what it revealed about the society that made it.
B. In 1974, farmers digging a well near Xi'an unearthed fragments of a life-sized terracotta figure.
C. Archaeologists soon realised that thousands of such figures lay buried, an entire army sculpted to guard an emperor in death.
D. Each soldier, it turned out, bore a distinct face, hinting at a scale of organised craftsmanship unprecedented for its age.
(1) BCDA  (2) BDCA  (3) CBDA  (4) BCAD  (5) DBCA
example.solution: B opens: it fixes the time (1974), place (Xi'an) and the first, singular find. C must follow B because 'such figures' needs the 'figure' in B as its antecedent, and 'soon realised' extends the discovery. D follows C because 'Each soldier' refers back to the 'army' introduced in C. A closes: 'However, the deeper significance...' is an evaluative contrast that caps the description. Order = B-C-D-A. Eliminate the rest: any order starting with C or D strands the pronouns 'such figures'/'each soldier' with no antecedent, and BCAD wrongly drops the reflective conclusion A before the descriptive detail D. Answer: (1) BCDA.

traps: Trusting a smooth-sounding local pair without checking that every pronoun and 'such/this' has an earlier antecedent -- the real test is global reference, not local flow.
Forgetting that 'however/but/yet' signals a turn, so that sentence cannot be the opener and rarely sits early.

subtopics.name: Grammar & sentence correction
subtopics.summary: Direct grammar is a live XAT item that CAT dropped entirely: you must spot errors or pick the grammatically correct/most-appropriate sentence, and it is scored as a 5-option MCQ. Recurring targets are subject-verb agreement (proximity and inversion rules), pronoun-antecedent agreement, misplaced/dangling modifiers, parallelism, tense sequence and idiomatic pairs like 'hardly...when', 'no sooner...than', 'not only...but also'.
keyIdeas: Nail the high-yield agreement rules: 'each/every/one of' takes a singular verb; with 'neither...nor / either...or' the verb agrees with the NEARER subject; 'the number of' is singular while 'a number of' is plural.
For correlative idioms memorise the fixed second half -- 'hardly/scarcely...WHEN', 'no sooner...THAN', 'not only...BUT ALSO' -- a wrong second half is an instant elimination.
Check that a pronoun agrees with its antecedent in number ('one of those people WHO are...' -- who refers to the plural 'people').
Test one sentence at a time and kill it on the first genuine error you can name; do not reward a sentence just because it 'sounds' fine.

example.q:
Which of the following sentences is grammatically correct?
(1) Each of the students have submitted their assignment on time.
(2) The committee have decided to postpone its meeting until next week.
(3) Hardly had he entered the room when the lights went off.
(4) He is one of those people who is always complaining.
(5) The number of applicants were higher than expected.
example.solution: (1) is wrong: 'Each of the students' is singular, so it needs 'has submitted', not 'have'. (2) is inconsistent: 'The committee have' (plural) clashes with 'its meeting' (singular). (4) is wrong: 'who' refers to the plural 'those people', so it must be 'who ARE always complaining'. (5) is wrong: 'The number of' takes a singular verb -- 'was', not 'were'. (3) is correct: the inverted correlative 'Hardly had he entered... WHEN the lights went off' uses the right pairing and past-perfect-then-past sequence. Answer: (3).

traps: 'Sounds right' is not a rule -- collective nouns, 'one of those who', and the number/a number distinction all defy the ear.
Mismatching the correlative halves (writing 'hardly...than' or 'no sooner...when') -- examiners plant exactly this swap.

subtopics.name: Fill in the blanks / cloze
subtopics.summary: Another CAT-dropped item that XAT still asks directly: one or two blanks to be filled with the option that fits meaning, tone and collocation. Single-blank items are usually vocabulary-in-context; double-blank items force a pair where BOTH words must fit, which is what makes elimination decisive.
keyIdeas: Read the whole sentence for its logical direction first -- look for signposts like 'so...that', 'however', 'even', 'although', 'however reluctantly' that tell you whether the tone is positive or negative.
In two-blank questions, reject an option the moment EITHER word fails -- you rarely need to judge both.
Watch collocation, not just dictionary meaning: 'incur a loss', 'pose a threat', 'cast doubt' -- the natural partner word often decides it.
Prefer the precise word over a vaguely-positive one; XAT distractors are usually 'nearly right' synonyms.

example.q:
Fill in the blanks with the most appropriate pair.
'The senator's speech was so ____ that even his staunchest critics found themselves ____ by his arguments, however reluctantly.'
(1) vapid ... persuaded
(2) cogent ... swayed
(3) rambling ... convinced
(4) eloquent ... alienated
(5) tedious ... captivated
example.solution: The frame 'so ____ that even his staunchest critics... however reluctantly' signals a speech powerful enough to win over hostile listeners against their will. Blank 1 needs a word meaning compelling/logical, and blank 2 a word meaning won over. (1) 'vapid' (dull) cannot cause persuasion. (3) 'rambling' contradicts a speech that convinces critics. (4) 'alienated' reverses the required sense -- critics were won over, not pushed away. (5) 'tedious' cannot 'captivate'. Only (2) 'cogent' (logically compelling) ... 'swayed' (influenced) fits both blanks. Answer: (2).

traps: Locking onto a good first word and ignoring that its partner in that option is wrong -- both must fit.
Choosing a positive-sounding word (eloquent, captivated) when a signpost like 'however reluctantly' demands the sense of grudging persuasion, not delight.

subtopics.name: Para-completion
subtopics.summary: You are given a paragraph with the last (occasionally the first) sentence missing and must choose the option that best completes it. XAT keeps this as a 5-option MCQ; the right answer continues the author's exact line of argument and tone rather than adding a true-but-unrelated fact.
keyIdeas: Track the paragraph's single main thread and pick the option that closes THAT thread -- the ending should feel inevitable, not merely acceptable.
Reject any option that opens a new topic, adds an off-thread fact, or narrows to a detail the passage never set up.
Match the connective: if the last line should conclude, the answer often begins with 'Thus/In this way/In the end'; if it should contrast, with 'Yet/However'.
Beware options that are factually true but tonally or logically foreign to the passage -- truth is not the test, continuity is.

example.q:
Choose the sentence that best completes the paragraph.
'For centuries, mapmakers filled the blank spaces of unexplored territory with imagined monsters and speculative coastlines. These embellishments were not mere decoration; they were admissions of ignorance dressed as knowledge. As exploration advanced, the monsters receded, replaced by the honest emptiness of the uncharted. ____'
(1) Thus, modern cartography owes its precision to satellite technology developed in the twentieth century.
(2) In this way, the blank space itself became a more truthful map than the crowded fantasies it replaced.
(3) Sea monsters, however, remain popular motifs in contemporary decorative art.
(4) Mapmakers, therefore, were among the least reliable chroniclers of their age.
(5) Exploration, as a result, was driven primarily by commercial rather than scientific motives.
example.solution: The paragraph builds one idea: the imagined monsters were 'ignorance dressed as knowledge', and progress replaced them with 'honest emptiness'. The closing line should crystallise that irony -- that emptiness is more honest than invented detail. (2) does exactly this. (1) veers into satellite technology, a new topic. (3) is a trivial aside about decorative art. (4) draws a sweeping character judgement the passage does not make. (5) discusses the motives of exploration, never raised. Answer: (2).

traps: Picking a statement that is true in the real world but does not continue the author's specific argument.
Choosing an over-broad moral or a narrow detail when the passage was building to a single, tightly-scoped conclusion.

subtopics.name: Para-summary
subtopics.summary: A short passage is followed by four or five candidate summaries, and you pick the one that captures the main point without distorting it. XAT's literary/abstract tilt makes the distractors subtle: the winner neither overstates ('always', 'never', 'proves', 'all') nor omits the passage's central claim.
keyIdeas: State the passage's core in your own one line first, then match it to an option -- do not let the options lead you.
Eliminate any summary with an extreme or absolute quantifier ('always', 'wrong', 'purely', 'in all cases') that the passage only hedged.
Reject options that grab a supporting detail or an example and treat it as the main point.
The best summary keeps the passage's scope and hedging intact -- 'as much as' should not become 'more than'.

example.q:
Choose the option that best captures the essence of the passage.
'Economists have long assumed that people make decisions to maximise their self-interest. Yet experiments repeatedly show that individuals will sacrifice personal gain to punish those they perceive as unfair, even at a cost to themselves. This willingness to incur a loss in the name of fairness cannot be explained by narrow self-interest alone; it suggests that our economic behaviour is shaped as much by moral intuition as by rational calculation.'
(1) Economists are wrong to build models, because human behaviour is fundamentally irrational and cannot be predicted.
(2) People always punish unfairness regardless of the personal cost, proving that fairness matters more than money.
(3) Experimental evidence that people will sacrifice gain to punish unfairness challenges the assumption that economic behaviour is driven purely by self-interest.
(4) Moral intuition is more important than rational calculation in all human decision-making.
(5) The goal of economics should be to study fairness rather than self-interest.
example.solution: The passage's core: costly punishment of unfairness is evidence that pure self-interest cannot fully explain economic behaviour, which is shaped 'as much by' morality as by calculation. (3) states exactly this, with matching scope. (1) overstates -- the passage never says models are worthless or behaviour unpredictable. (2) overstates with 'always...regardless' and 'proving...more than'. (4) inflates 'as much as' into 'more important...in all decision-making'. (5) is a prescription the passage never makes. Answer: (3).

traps: Choosing the boldest-sounding option -- summary answers reward faithful scope, and the strong-worded choice is usually the trap.
Confusing a vivid detail (the punishment experiments) for the thesis (self-interest is an incomplete explanation).

subtopics.name: Vocabulary (synonyms, antonyms, idioms & usage)
subtopics.summary: Standalone vocabulary is a live XAT item that current CAT no longer tests: expect direct synonyms/antonyms of hard words, the meaning of an idiom, and word-usage questions (which sentence uses a word correctly). It rewards a genuinely built lexicon plus context-reading, since the distractors are near-synonyms with the wrong shade.
keyIdeas: Learn idioms as whole units with their figurative meaning -- literal decoding fails ('put the cat among the pigeons' has nothing to do with cats).
For synonym/antonym items, judge the word's connotation (positive/negative) and register first; that alone eliminates two or three near-misses.
In word-usage questions, plug the word into each sentence and check collocation and part of speech, not just broad meaning.
Use word roots and the sentence's tone signposts to pin the exact shade when two options look close.

example.q:
Select the option that best expresses the meaning of the idiom in italics:
'The new disclosure norms *put the cat among the pigeons* at the annual shareholders' meeting.'
(1) restored a sense of calm and order
(2) caused disruption and provoked anxiety
(3) revealed a long-hidden secret
(4) favoured the powerful over the weak
(5) went completely unnoticed
example.solution: 'Put the cat among the pigeons' means to say or do something that causes trouble, alarm or heated disagreement -- picture a cat loosed into a flock, scattering it in panic. In context, the new norms threw the meeting into turmoil. (1) is the opposite; (3) 'revealed a secret' and (4) 'favoured the powerful' are unrelated senses people guess at; (5) reverses the meaning. Answer: (2) caused disruption and provoked anxiety.

traps: Decoding an idiom literally or mapping it to a similar-sounding but different idiom.
Picking a near-synonym with the wrong connotation -- XAT's wrong options are deliberately 'almost right'.

subtopics.name: Analogies & word relationships
subtopics.summary: Word analogies -- 'A : B :: ? : ?' -- are a live XAT item that CAT dropped: you must identify the precise relationship in the given pair (function, part-whole, cause-effect, degree, category) and find the option with the SAME relationship in the same direction. Precision beats plausibility, because two options are usually related to the answer domain and only one shares the exact relationship.
keyIdeas: Name the relationship in a full sentence first ('a gill is the organ through which a fish breathes'), then test each option against that exact sentence.
Keep the direction consistent: if the given pair is organ-to-animal, the answer must also be organ-to-animal, not animal-to-organ.
Distinguish the relationship TYPE -- function vs. part-whole vs. degree -- because distractors often share the domain but not the type (fin:shark is locomotion, not respiration).
When two options survive, choose the one whose relationship is tightest and most defining, not merely associated.

example.q:
Choose the pair that best expresses a relationship similar to that in the given pair.
GILL : FISH
(1) trunk : elephant
(2) lung : mammal
(3) root : plant
(4) fin : shark
(5) leaf : tree
example.solution: The defining relationship is 'a gill is the respiratory organ by which a fish breathes'. Apply that test: (2) 'a lung is the respiratory organ by which a mammal breathes' -- an exact match in type and direction. (1) the elephant's trunk is chiefly for grasping/smelling, not the defining respiratory organ. (3) a root absorbs water, not a breathing organ. (4) a fin is for locomotion. (5) a leaf's primary role is photosynthesis, not respiration, and the fit is looser. The tightest, same-type, same-direction pair is (2). Answer: (2) lung : mammal.

traps: Settling for a pair from the same domain (all these are body parts of animals/plants) instead of matching the exact relationship.
Reversing the direction of the relationship, or accepting a loose association ('leaf:tree') over the defining function ('lung:mammal').

subtopics.name: Strengthen & weaken arguments
subtopics.summary: A short argument (a few premises leading to a conclusion) is followed by 'which of the following, if true, most strengthens/weakens it'. Within XAT's small but nuanced Critical Reasoning share, this is the single most common argument type, and it also appears embedded inside the more abstract/philosophical RC passages.
keyIdeas: First isolate the conclusion, then find the unstated leap between the evidence and that conclusion — the right answer acts on that leap, not on a premise you can already see.
Weaken by supplying an alternative explanation, a counterexample, or a broken assumption; strengthen by ruling an alternative out or confirming the missing link.
Check scope and relevance: the option must bite on the exact conclusion (its subject, its time frame, its quantifier). Discard options that are true but change nothing.
Pick the option with the strongest logical impact, not the one that merely sounds on-topic; 'most weakens' is comparative.

example.q:
A city launched a bike-sharing programme last year. Since then, traffic congestion in the downtown core has fallen by 15%. The transport department concludes that the bike-sharing programme caused the reduction in congestion.

Which of the following, if true, most weakens the department's conclusion?
(A) The bike-sharing programme proved more popular than the department had predicted.
(B) In the same year, the city closed two major downtown streets to cars and doubled parking fees in the core.
(C) Bike-sharing programmes in two comparable cities were also followed by lower congestion.
(D) The 15% reduction was measured with the same methodology used in previous years.
(E) Some residents complained that the new bike docks occupy pavement space.
example.solution: The conclusion is causal: programme -> congestion drop. (B) introduces a confounding cause operating in the very same period — street closures plus doubled parking fees would by themselves push cars out of the core — so the 15% drop can no longer be pinned on the bikes. That directly severs the evidence-to-conclusion link, so (B) is the answer. (A) popularity of the scheme does not show it caused the traffic change; (C) an analogous case actually strengthens the causal story; (D) consistent methodology bolsters the data's reliability (mild strengthen); (E) pavement complaints are irrelevant to congestion. Only (B) attacks the causal leap.

traps: Attacking a stated premise instead of the reasoning gap — the question asks you to assume the premises are true.
Choosing an option that is merely on-topic or emotionally resonant but leaves the conclusion untouched.
Ignoring 'most': two options may both nudge the argument, but only one decisively swings it.

subtopics.name: Assumptions & necessary premises
subtopics.summary: An assumption is the unstated premise the argument must take for granted for its conclusion to hold. XAT phrases it as 'the argument relies on which assumption' or 'which is taken for granted', and rewards spotting the minimal bridge — not a bold new claim and not a restatement of what is already given.
keyIdeas: Use the negation test: negate the option; if the argument falls apart, that option is a necessary assumption; if the argument survives, it is not.
A real assumption fills the gap between a premise and the conclusion — it links terms that appear on only one side of the argument.
XAT wants necessary (must-be-true) assumptions, which are minimal and cautious; reject options that overstate or that would be merely sufficient.
Beware options that simply repeat a premise or add outside information the argument never needed.

example.q:
Editor: 'Our magazine should switch to online-only publication. Printing and distribution are our two largest costs, so eliminating them will make the magazine profitable.'

The editor's argument depends on which of the following assumptions?
(A) Printing and distribution together account for more than half of the magazine's total costs.
(B) Going online-only will not lose so much subscription and advertising revenue that the cost savings are wiped out.
(C) Online-only magazines are more environmentally friendly than printed ones.
(D) The magazine's main competitors have already moved online.
(E) Most readers prefer reading on a screen to reading on paper.
example.solution: The conclusion (going online-only makes the magazine profitable) rests on cutting print costs, but profit depends on revenue too. Apply the negation test to (B): if going online-only DID lose enough revenue to wipe out the savings, the magazine would not become profitable and the conclusion collapses — so (B) is a necessary assumption and is the answer. (A) is not required: the stem only says these are the two largest costs, and profitability does not hinge on them exceeding half. (C) environmental impact is irrelevant to profit. (D) competitors' behaviour is not needed for this magazine's own cost/revenue math. (E) even if most readers prefer paper the magazine could still be profitable, so it is not necessary. Only (B) survives negation.

traps: Picking an option that would help the argument (sufficient) rather than one the argument cannot do without (necessary).
Choosing a statement that merely restates a given premise instead of bridging premise and conclusion.
Over-selecting strong, sweeping claims — necessary assumptions are usually cautious and narrow.

subtopics.name: Conclusion & inference
subtopics.summary: Two related tasks: identify the argument's main conclusion (the claim everything else supports), or select what 'can be logically concluded / must be true' from the statements. XAT overlaps this with RC inference and its answers reward what the text guarantees, not what merely sounds plausible.
keyIdeas: A valid inference is fully entailed by the statements — a small guaranteed step; if you must import outside information, the option is wrong.
Translate conditional statements (all/if-then, none/no) and chain them, including contrapositives, before scanning the options.
Distrust extreme quantifiers (all, none, only, must) unless the text is itself that categorical; among survivors prefer the more modest, hedged claim.
For 'main conclusion', strip away the support ('because', 'since') and keep the one claim the rest is marshalled to prove.

example.q:
Every employee who received a performance bonus this year also attended the leadership workshop. No one who joined the company after March attended the leadership workshop.

Which of the following can be logically concluded?
(A) Every employee who attended the leadership workshop received a performance bonus.
(B) No employee who joined the company after March received a performance bonus.
(C) Some employees who joined before March received a performance bonus.
(D) Everyone who attended the leadership workshop joined the company before March.
(E) All employees attended the leadership workshop.
example.solution: Encode the two facts: bonus -> attended workshop; joined-after-March -> did NOT attend. Chain them: anyone who joined after March did not attend, and no attendance means no bonus, so joined-after-March -> no bonus. That is exactly (B), the answer. (A) reverses the first conditional (attending does not guarantee a bonus) — illicit conversion. (C) is unsupported: possibly no one received a bonus at all. (D) overreaches — 'not after March' includes people who joined IN March, so an attendee need not have joined strictly before March. (E) is not guaranteed by anything given. Only (B) is entailed.

traps: Confusing the converse ('attended -> bonus') or inverse with the valid contrapositive.
Selecting a real-world-plausible statement the text does not actually license.
Reading 'not after March' as 'before March' and losing the March-joiners edge case.

subtopics.name: Cause and effect
subtopics.summary: The stem observes a correlation or a sequence and leaps to a causal claim ('X caused Y'); the question asks what best explains, strengthens, or weakens that claim. XAT loves the classic causal fallacies — correlation mistaken for causation, a hidden third factor, and reverse causation — and buries them in otherwise reasonable-sounding arguments.
keyIdeas: When a causal claim rests on a correlation, test three rivals: an alternative common cause, reverse causation (the arrow runs the other way), and mere coincidence.
Weaken a causal claim by showing the effect appears without the cause, the cause without the effect, or a third factor driving both.
Strengthen it by ruling alternatives out, or by showing correct temporal order or a dose-response pattern (more cause, more effect).
Keep the exact causal direction of the conclusion in view — the best weakener often just flips that arrow.

example.q:
A study found that employees who take more vacation days receive higher performance ratings. The researchers concluded that taking more vacation improves job performance.

Which of the following, if true, most seriously undermines the researchers' conclusion?
(A) Some employees do not use all of their allotted vacation days.
(B) At this company, high-performing employees are rewarded with extra vacation days, which they then take.
(C) Employees who take more vacation report feeling less stressed at work.
(D) The performance ratings were assigned by managers who did not know how many vacation days each employee had taken.
(E) The study drew employees from several different departments.
example.solution: The conclusion assumes vacation -> better performance. (B) reverses that arrow: being a high performer is what earns the extra vacation, so the correlation is fully explained without vacation improving anything — this is textbook reverse causation and the strongest attack, so (B) is the answer. (C) offers a plausible mechanism (less stress) and if anything supports the researchers' direction. (D) blinding the raters removes a bias and strengthens the correlation's credibility. (A) unused days and (E) departmental spread do not bear on the direction of causation. Only (B) undoes the causal claim.

traps: Accepting a strong correlation as proof of causation without checking the direction of the arrow.
Mistaking an option that supplies a supporting mechanism for one that weakens the claim.
Overlooking a lurking third variable that produces both the supposed cause and the supposed effect.

subtopics.name: Statement–argument / course of action
subtopics.summary: A statement (usually describing a problem) is followed by proposed arguments to judge 'strong vs weak', or by courses of action to judge which 'logically follow'. This item is more central to Banking/SNAP-type tests than to XAT, but the same evaluative reasoning surfaces in XAT's Decision-Making-flavoured Critical Reasoning, so it is worth drilling lightly.
keyIdeas: A strong argument is directly relevant, substantial, and practical; a weak one is trivial, off-topic, merely restates the statement, or leans on a hypothetical or single example.
A course of action follows only if the problem is genuine AND the action plausibly and proportionately addresses it.
Reject extremes on both ends — the do-nothing/panic option and the sweeping shut-it-all-down option; XLRI-style reasoning favours measured, workable responses.
Evaluate each option independently on relevance and feasibility before you settle on the combination.

example.q:
Statement: Many commuters complain that the city's metro trains are dangerously overcrowded during peak morning hours.
Courses of action:
I. The metro authority should increase the frequency of trains during peak morning hours.
II. The metro authority should permanently shut down the metro service to prevent overcrowding accidents.
III. The metro authority should study peak-hour ridership data and add coaches on the most crowded routes.

Which course(s) of action logically follow(s)?
(A) Only I follows
(B) Only III follows
(C) Both I and III follow
(D) Only II follows
(E) All of I, II and III follow
example.solution: Judge each action on relevance and feasibility. I raises capacity during exactly the problem window, so more peak trains directly relieve overcrowding — it follows. III adds capacity too, and does so proportionately by targeting the worst routes using data — it follows. II 'solves' overcrowding by abolishing the service itself; that is an extreme, self-defeating action that abandons the commuters' actual need, so it does not follow. With I and III valid and II rejected, the answer is (C). (A) and (B) each drop a valid action; (D) and (E) wrongly keep the extreme option II.

traps: Marking an action 'strong' just because it sounds decisive, without checking it actually fits the stated problem.
Accepting a drastic or alarmist course (shut it down, ban it entirely) that overreaches the issue.
Rejecting a sound action because it is only a partial or first step — a relevant, feasible step still 'follows'.

sections.name: Decision Making
sections.format: ~7 caselets x ~3 questions; unique to XAT; judgment-based, no syllabus

subtopics.name: Stakeholder mapping (identify every affected party)
subtopics.summary: The foundational DM skill: before evaluating options, list every party the decision touches — the protagonist, the person acted upon, the wider team, customers, the organization, sometimes society. XAT builds each caselet around several named stakeholders and rewards the option that accounts for ALL of them, not just the decision-maker's convenience.
keyIdeas: On first read, mentally underline or list every party named or implied in the caselet — a stakeholder you forget is usually the one the correct option protects.
Score each option by how many stakeholders it serves: options that satisfy only the protagonist (or only one loud party) are almost always the wrong answer.
The XLRI-approved option typically names or addresses the stakeholder a hurried reader would overlook (juniors watching, a customer downstream, a family situation).

example.q:
Ravi manages a mid-size textile unit. Meena, a long-serving supervisor, has arrived late for two weeks because she now cares for her ailing mother at home each morning. Her lateness is delaying the morning shift's output, and junior workers have begun grumbling that the rules seem to apply unequally. Ravi must respond. What should he do?
(a) Issue Meena a formal written warning to reassert that the rules apply to everyone.
(b) Quietly overlook the lateness, since Meena has a genuine personal reason.
(c) Speak privately with Meena to agree a temporary flexible-start arrangement, and briefly explain to the shift that a need-based exception is being made, so juniors see it as compassion, not favouritism.
(d) Move Meena to a role with no fixed start time and dock her seniority pay.
(e) Let the junior workers also start late so the timing feels equal to everyone.
example.solution: Map the stakeholders: (1) Meena and her genuine constraint, (2) the junior workers who are watching for fairness, and (3) the unit's morning output. Now test each option against all three. (a) serves only the juniors' fairness and abandons Meena. (b) serves only Meena and ignores both the grumbling juniors and the delayed output. (d) punishes Meena and fixes nothing for the team's perception. (e) protects fairness-optics by degrading output for everyone — it serves no one well. Only (c) speaks to all three parties at once: Meena gets accommodation, the juniors get a transparent reason that removes the favouritism sting, and output is managed through a defined arrangement. The option that covers the full stakeholder set is the answer.

traps: The single-stakeholder option — one that fully satisfies the loudest or most sympathetic party (here (a) for the rule-followers or (b) for Meena) while silently dropping everyone else. It feels decisive but fails the map.

subtopics.name: Balanced / win-win option selection
subtopics.summary: Once stakeholders are mapped, XAT expects you to prefer the option that advances competing interests together rather than trading one off against another. The correct answer usually reframes a seemingly either/or situation into a solution where both sides gain something real. Zero-sum 'pick a side' options are planted decoys.
keyIdeas: When two options each fully please one party at the other's expense, treat both as decoys and hunt for a third that reshapes the deliverable so both parties get a genuine (if partial) win.
A real win-win must be concrete and honest — not a vague 'satisfy everyone' promise and not a hidden compromise that quietly shortchanges one side.
Ask 'does this leave a lasting workable relationship?' — the balanced option preserves the relationship, the zero-sum option leaves a loser.

example.q:
A software firm's important client demands a feature two weeks early to launch it at a trade show. The engineering lead says the only way to hit that date is to skip the testing cycle, which risks visible bugs on stage. The account manager is desperate to keep the client; the engineering lead insists on product quality. What is the best course?
(a) Deliver on the client's early date without full testing, to keep the client happy.
(b) Refuse the early date and hold the original timeline to protect quality.
(c) Offer the client a stable, fully tested subset of the feature — the parts that can be verified in time — for the trade show, with the complete feature to follow shortly after, and be transparent about the scope.
(d) Deliver everything on the early date but quietly tell the client's staff to avoid demoing the risky parts on stage.
(e) Ask the client to postpone their trade show.
example.solution: Options (a) and (b) are the classic zero-sum pair: (a) hands the account manager a win by sacrificing engineering's quality; (b) hands engineering a win by sacrificing the client. Each has a clear loser, so both are decoys. (d) reaches for a fake win-win by concealment — it is deceptive and collapses the moment a bug appears. (e) simply overrides the client's real constraint. Option (c) is the genuine win-win: the client gets something reliable to demo on their date, engineering ships nothing untested, and transparency keeps trust intact. It converts the either/or (full-but-risky vs late-but-safe) into a tested-subset-now, full-feature-soon deliverable that lets both parties gain.

traps: The two 'take a side' options ((a) and (b)) — each is fully satisfying to one stakeholder, which is exactly what makes it tempting and wrong.
The counterfeit win-win that looks like it pleases both sides but relies on hiding something (d) — a balanced answer never buys agreement with concealment.

subtopics.name: Ethics-first filter
subtopics.summary: Ethics is a gate, not a tiebreaker: before comparing options on merit, strike out any that involve dishonesty, bribery, concealment, cutting a legal or safety corner, or pure profit at another's cost — however attractive the numbers look. XAT reliably includes a profitable-but-unethical option AND a subtler option that is personally clean but not transparent.
keyIdeas: Run the ethics filter FIRST and eliminate, before you weigh cost, speed, or convenience — an option that fails ethics never re-enters, no matter how efficient it is.

Distinguish two failures: the blatant one (accept the bribe, hide the defect) and the quiet one (personally decline the wrong but stay silent) — XLRI's answer usually demands active transparency, not just private abstention.
The ethical answer disclose-and-let-the-proper-process-decide often beats both 'take the gain' and 'over-punish', because it removes the decision-maker's conflict without harming innocent parties.

example.q:
Anil, a procurement officer, is evaluating bids. He discovers that the lowest bidder — whose product is genuinely good and cheapest on merit — has quietly offered him a personal 'consulting fee' if selected. Anil's performance targets reward cost savings. What should he do?
(a) Award the bid on merit (it truly is cheapest) and quietly decline the personal fee.
(b) Accept both the bid and the fee, since that bidder would have won on merit anyway.
(c) Disclose the offered fee to the procurement committee, recuse himself, and let the committee evaluate all bids on documented merit.
(d) Reject this bidder outright to avoid any appearance of impropriety, and pick the next-cheapest bidder.
(e) Report the bidder to the police for attempted bribery and blacklist the firm on the spot.
example.solution: The ethics filter instantly removes (b) — that is a bribe. The sharper test is (a): it feels ethical because Anil turns the money down, yet he stays the decision-maker on a bid where his integrity has been compromised, and he conceals the attempt — private virtue with no transparency. (d) over-corrects by punishing the organization: it throws away a genuinely cheapest, good product because of Anil's discomfort, harming an innocent employer. (e) is a disproportionate, unilateral escalation. (c) is the XLRI answer: Anil neither profits nor hides. He surfaces the conflict, steps out of the decision, and lets an untainted process still judge the good bid on its merits — ethical AND fair to the deserving bidder and the employer.

traps: The profitable-and-defensible bribe (b) — 'they'd have won anyway' is the rationalization the filter is built to reject.

The 'quietly decline' option (a) — the trap for careful readers, because refusing the money feels sufficient; ethics-first demands disclosure and removal of the conflict, not silent personal cleanliness.

subtopics.name: Avoid extremes (harsh and passive both trap)
subtopics.summary: XAT almost always seeds two opposite decoys in every caselet: the harshest option (fire, sue, punish, shut it down) and the passive do-nothing option (ignore it, wait, 'sales are up so leave it'). The expected answer sits in the reasoned middle — it acts on the problem without an overreaction and without abdicating. Recognizing the two poles is half the work.
keyIdeas: After reading the options, tag the single harshest and the single most passive — then set both aside as likely traps before evaluating the rest.
The correct middle option still ACTS (passivity is not moderation) but its action is proportionate: correct the process, cap the risk, pilot the good part.

Beware a 'moderate-looking' punishment (transfer, demote) that is really a disguised extreme or a face-saving dodge — proportionality means matching the response to the actual facts, not softening a punishment.

example.q:
A retail chain's regional head learns that one store manager, Prakash, has been letting staff give unauthorized discounts to loyal regulars. Sales at his store are up 12%, but margins are down, and he never sought approval. Head office is annoyed. What should the regional head do?
(a) Terminate Prakash for repeatedly violating pricing policy.
(b) Do nothing — overall sales are up, so leave it alone.
(c) Immediately roll back all discounts and issue a company-wide warning about pricing discipline.
(d) Ask Prakash to document which discounts actually drove repeat business, cap discretionary discounts within an approved limit, and pilot the effective ones as an official loyalty scheme.
(e) Transfer Prakash to a smaller store to send a signal.
example.solution: Identify the poles first. (a) is the harsh extreme — firing a manager who, unauthorized as he was, uncovered something partly working. (b) is the passive extreme — an unapproved practice and margin erosion left to continue because the top line looks nice. Both are decoys. (c) is also on the harsh side: it kills a working loyalty driver and punishes the whole chain for one store's issue. (e) is the disguised half-punishment — it 'signals' without addressing the actual question of whether the discounting has value. (d) is the proportionate middle: it stops the unauthorized behaviour by imposing approval limits (so the process is fixed) while capturing the genuine loyalty gain through a sanctioned scheme. It acts, but neither overreacts nor abdicates.

traps: The 'fire him' option (a) — bait for readers who anchor on the rule-breaking and want it punished hardest.
The 'do nothing, sales are up' option (b) — bait for readers who anchor on results; passivity masquerading as tolerance.

subtopics.name: Long-term & sustainable over short-term fixes
subtopics.summary: When a caselet describes a recurring or systemic problem, XAT rewards the option that fixes the root cause durably over the option that patches the immediate symptom. The short-term fix is engineered to look responsive and cheap; the tell is that the underlying problem will simply return next cycle.
keyIdeas: Ask 'will this same problem recur next month/quarter?' — if the option leaves the root cause intact, it is a patch, not a solution, no matter how quick it feels.
The sustainable answer often costs a little more or moves a little slower up front but is phased/funded so it fits real constraints — prefer it over the reactive, band-aid option.
Cosmetic or deferral options ('deep-clean for the season', 'announce we'll fix it next year') are symptom-management in disguise — treat them as short-term traps.

example.q:
A city bus company suffers frequent breakdowns from its ageing fleet, worst just as ridership peaks. The festival rush is a month away. Money is tight. The recurring cause is years of deferred maintenance across the whole fleet. What should management decide?
(a) Lease a few replacement buses for the festival rush and revisit maintenance later.
(b) Do a one-time deep-clean so the buses look presentable through the season.
(c) Launch a phased, funded preventive-maintenance program across the fleet, starting with the worst-affected buses now, even if that means leasing only a couple of buses for the rush.
(d) Raise fares to pay for repairs reactively as buses break down.
(e) Announce that new buses will be purchased next year and keep running the fleet as-is until then.
example.solution: The stated cause is systemic — deferred maintenance across the fleet — so the breakdowns will keep recurring until that is addressed. (a) is the seductive short-term patch: it covers the one-month rush and feels responsive, but the fleet still fails the following month. (b) is purely cosmetic. (d) treats symptoms reactively and pushes cost onto riders without touching the cause. (e) defers the real work by a year while the breakdowns continue. Only (c) attacks the root cause with a phased plan sized to the tight budget — it accepts a smaller short-term concession (leasing just a couple of buses) in exchange for a durable fix. Sustainable over quick beats the patch.

traps: The quick-patch option (a) — it 'solves' the visible immediate deadline and looks prudent, but leaves the recurring cause untouched so the problem returns.

subtopics.name: Read only what is stated (no outside assumptions)
subtopics.summary: The discipline that ties the others together: judge the caselet strictly on the facts given — no imported motives, no assumed guilt or innocence, no real-world knowledge the passage did not supply. XAT deliberately plants options that are only correct IF you assume a fact that was never stated. Reject them and prefer the option that acts on the stated facts (often by verifying the gap) rather than filling the gap with a guess.
keyIdeas: Before accepting any option, ask 'does choosing this require me to assume something the caselet never told me?' — if yes, it is almost certainly the trap.
Do not read in malice OR innocence: if the passage states an ambiguous fact, the right option gathers the missing information rather than concluding either way.
Your outside expertise is not evidence — a claim that would be true in the real world but is unstated in the caselet cannot justify an option.

example.q:
Sunita, a team lead, notices that a report submitted by a new hire, Rohit, contains figures identical to those in a competitor's published white paper. Asked about it, Rohit says he 'used public benchmarks.' The caselet tells you only these facts. What should Sunita do?
(a) Dismiss Rohit for plagiarism — copying a competitor's figures is clearly theft.
(b) Ask Rohit to walk her through his sources and methodology for the figures, and compare them against the white paper before reaching any conclusion.
(c) Report Rohit to HR for an intellectual-property violation.
(d) Ignore it — new hires often lean on published data, so it's fine.
(e) Warn Rohit privately that he must be more careful about how things look.
example.solution: List only what is stated: the figures match, and Rohit claims they came from public benchmarks. What is NOT stated: that Rohit copied dishonestly, that the figures are proprietary, or that the numbers are wrong — public benchmark data can legitimately be identical across reports. (a) and (c) both leap to 'plagiarism'/'IP violation', conclusions the caselet never establishes; they require an assumption of guilt. (d) assumes innocence ('it's fine') just as baselessly. (e) assumes there is something to be guilty about ('how things look'). Only (b) stays inside the stated facts: it treats the match as an open question and gathers the missing information — sources and methodology — before judging. When the passage leaves a gap, verify; do not invent.

traps: The assume-guilt options ((a) and (c)) — they read 'theft' and 'IP violation' into a situation the caselet describes only as identical figures with a plausible public-data explanation.
The assume-innocence option (d) — the mirror-image trap that fills the same gap with a comforting guess instead of checking the stated facts.

subtopics.name: Honesty vs loyalty conflicts
subtopics.summary: Caselets where being truthful (to a client, employer, or the public) collides with loyalty to a friend, mentor, boss, or team who did something wrong or wants a fault hidden. XAT tests whether you protect the truth without needlessly destroying the relationship — the credited answer is almost never 'stay silent' and almost never 'publicly expose'.
keyIdeas: Identify the two goods in tension: honesty (owed to the wronged stakeholder) and loyalty (owed to the person). The XLRI answer honours BOTH, it does not sacrifice one entirely.
Look for the option that fixes the untruth AND gives the loyal party the first, dignified chance to correct it themselves before it is escalated.
Loyalty never means concealing a lie or a harm — but it does mean not humiliating a colleague when a quieter correction achieves the same honest outcome.
Rank options: (i) conceal = fails honesty, (ii) expose harshly = fails loyalty/proportion, (iii) privately confront + insist on correction = balanced winner.

example.q:
Rohan discovers that Anita — his manager and long-time mentor — overstated projected savings in a report already sent to an important client, who has not noticed. Anita, who is up for promotion, privately asks Rohan to let it pass, calling it 'a rounding matter'. Rohan believes the error is material. What should he do?
(a) Stay silent, because Anita mentored him and he owes her his loyalty.
(b) Email the client the correction and copy senior management, so the error is on record.
(c) Tell Anita privately that he cannot be part of concealing a material error, and press her to issue a corrected report to the client herself.
(d) Anonymously tip the client off about the discrepancy.
(e) Do nothing now, but quietly keep a copy of the error to protect himself if it surfaces later.
example.solution: Correct answer: (c). Honesty is owed to the client, who is acting on wrong numbers, so silence (a) and self-protective inaction (e) both fail the truth. But XAT rewards proportion and loyalty too: (b) exposes Anita to senior management before she has any chance to fix her own mistake, and (d) is itself dishonest and sneaky. Option (c) is the balanced, stakeholder-inclusive path — it guarantees the client gets the truth while giving the mentor the first, dignified opportunity to correct the record herself, preserving the relationship and long-term trust. It is honest without being vindictive.
options: Stay silent out of loyalty to his mentor
Email the client the correction and copy senior management
Privately tell Anita he cannot conceal it and press her to issue a corrected report
Anonymously tip off the client
Do nothing but keep a copy to protect himself later

traps: The 'loyalty' option (stay silent for a mentor/friend) is emotionally tempting but concealing a material error is dishonest — it is a distractor, not the answer.
The 'expose and CC the bosses' option feels principled but is the harsh extreme; XAT prefers giving the wrongdoer a chance to self-correct first.
Any anonymous or covert route (tip-offs, secret records) is treated as dishonest even when the underlying goal is good.

subtopics.name: Whistleblowing & reporting wrongdoing
subtopics.summary: Caselets where an employee uncovers genuine wrongdoing — a safety defect, fraud, or violation — and must decide whether and how to report it. XAT's expected answer follows a proportionate escalation ladder: raise it internally with evidence first, escalate up the chain if ignored, and reserve external routes (media, regulator) for when internal channels genuinely fail or lives are at immediate risk.
keyIdeas: Distinguish the DUTY (the wrong must be addressed — ignoring it is never credited) from the METHOD (how loudly, how publicly, in what order).
Prefer documented internal escalation with evidence over both silence and a straight-to-media leak; the credited option usually keeps the wrongdoing from proceeding while using proper channels.
Weigh urgency: the greater and more immediate the harm to the public/stakeholders, the more forceful and prompt the action the answer justifies.
Resigning 'in protest' or 'to keep a clean conscience' is a do-nothing trap — it abandons the very stakeholders at risk.

example.q:
Meera, a quality engineer at a pharmaceutical firm, finds test data showing a batch is contaminated in a way that could harm patients. The batch is scheduled to ship tomorrow. Her immediate supervisor waves her off, saying the deviation is 'within tolerance'. Meera is convinced it is not. What is the most appropriate course of action?
(a) Ship the batch as instructed, since her supervisor has overruled her.
(b) Immediately send the test data to a national newspaper to force a recall.
(c) Quietly hold back the shipment herself without informing anyone.
(d) Resign in protest so she is not associated with an unsafe product.
(e) Escalate in writing to the plant head and the compliance/quality function, attaching the test evidence and formally requesting that the shipment be held pending review.
example.solution: Correct answer: (e). Patient safety is the paramount stakeholder interest, so shipping on orders (a) is unethical obedience, and resigning (d) is a do-nothing option that leaves patients exposed. Going straight to the press (b) is the disproportionate extreme — it skips the internal channels that could stop the batch faster and more accurately. Acting unilaterally and secretly (c) sidesteps accountability and could be reversed by others unaware of the risk. Option (e) is the balanced answer: it forces the issue up a documented internal chain with hard evidence and seeks a hold before harm occurs — decisive about the danger, proportionate about the method, and protective of the public.
options: Ship the batch as instructed
Send the test data to a national newspaper immediately
Quietly hold the shipment herself without telling anyone
Resign in protest
Escalate in writing with evidence to the plant head and compliance, requesting a hold

traps: The media-leak option looks brave and decisive, but XAT penalises skipping internal escalation when a faster, more accurate internal fix is available.
Resigning or refusing to be 'associated' with the wrong feels ethical but abandons the stakeholders — it is a disguised do-nothing.
Obeying a superior's dismissal is never a defence when public safety is at stake.

subtopics.name: Conflicts of interest & moonlighting
subtopics.summary: Caselets where a person's private interest — a relative's firm, a personal financial stake, a second job or side business — could bias, or appear to bias, a professional decision they control. XAT rewards disclosure and recusal (transparency plus stepping back) over both self-dealing and over-correction, and treats a hidden conflict as a fault even when the biased choice would have been 'correct' anyway.
keyIdeas: The core issue is not just actual bias but the APPEARANCE of bias — the fix is to make the conflict visible and remove yourself from the decision, not to argue you were objective.
Disclose to the right authority and recuse; let an independent party decide on merit. This protects the process, the organisation, and even the connected party.
'It was the best bid / choice anyway' is a rationalisation, not a defence — a concealed conflict taints the process regardless of outcome.
For moonlighting: the test is disclosure, absence of competing/company-time use, and no drain on the primary duty — secret side-work that uses employer resources or competes is the fault.

example.q:
Vikram, a procurement manager, is finalising a supplier contract. Of three shortlisted bidders, the strongest on both technical merit and price is a startup founded by his brother-in-law. What should Vikram do?
(a) Award the contract to his brother-in-law's firm, since it is objectively the best bid.
(b) Automatically disqualify his brother-in-law's firm to avoid any hint of favouritism.
(c) Disclose the relationship to his superior and the procurement committee, recuse himself from the final decision, and let an independent panel evaluate all three bids on merit.
(d) Ask his brother-in-law to withdraw the bid so the awkwardness disappears.
(e) Award the contract but keep detailed notes to justify the choice if anyone questions it later.
example.solution: Correct answer: (c). Deciding it himself — even for the genuinely best bid (a) — leaves a concealed conflict that taints the whole process, and keeping private justification notes (e) is the same self-dealing dressed up. But over-correcting by disqualifying (b) or pressuring the firm to withdraw (d) is unfair to the strongest bidder and simply swaps favouritism for prejudice. Option (c) is the transparent, balanced answer: disclosure plus recusal removes both the bias and the appearance of it, lets merit decide through an independent panel, and is fair to every stakeholder — including the relative's firm. It protects the integrity of the process, which is the long-term interest.
options: Award it to the brother-in-law's firm because it is the best bid
Automatically disqualify the brother-in-law's firm
Disclose the relationship, recuse himself, and let an independent panel decide on merit
Ask his brother-in-law to withdraw the bid
Award it but keep notes to justify himself later

traps: 'It was genuinely the best bid' is the seductive trap — a good outcome does not cure a hidden conflict of interest.
Auto-disqualifying or forcing a withdrawal looks scrupulous but is an unfair over-correction against the best bidder.
Documenting a self-made decision 'in case questions arise' is not transparency — real transparency means disclosing and stepping aside before deciding.

subtopics.name: Confidentiality vs transparency
subtopics.summary: Caselets where information a person is duty-bound to keep confidential (a restructuring plan, an insider fact, private data) would, if shared, help someone or prevent harm — but sharing breaches a legitimate obligation. XAT rewards the middle path: honour the confidentiality without lying, protect the affected person through honest general guidance, and push to shorten or resolve the harmful secrecy at its source.
keyIdeas: Separate 'must not reveal the specific confidential fact' from 'may still avoid deceiving someone' — you can decline to disclose without actively lying.
The credited option usually protects the vulnerable person indirectly (a general caution) while respecting the confidentiality obligation to everyone else.
Attack the root of the tension: urge the organisation to communicate sooner and support those affected, so the corrosive secrecy is shortened, not just endured.
Both extremes lose: breaching to 'warn a friend' is unfair to others and breaks trust; cold, mechanical silence that lets avoidable harm happen is the do-nothing failure.

example.q:
During a confidential restructuring, HR manager Sneha learns a division will close in three months and its 40 staff will be laid off. A close colleague from that division, unaware of the plan, privately asks her whether he should sign an expensive 20-year home loan this week. Company policy forbids disclosing the restructuring. What should she do?
(a) Quietly tell him about the coming layoff, since he is a friend who deserves to know.
(b) Without revealing the confidential plan, gently advise him to be cautious about any large, long-term financial commitment right now, and separately press management to communicate the restructuring and support affected staff sooner.
(c) Reassure him that everything is stable and encourage him to go ahead with the loan.
(d) Refuse to discuss it at all and change the subject.
(e) Anonymously leak the restructuring plan so everyone in the division can prepare.
example.solution: Correct answer: (b). Telling him outright (a) or leaking to all (e) breaches a legitimate confidentiality duty and is unfair to the many others equally affected. Reassuring him to take the loan (c) is an outright lie that actively harms him. Flatly refusing and changing the subject (d) keeps the secret but coldly lets an avoidable, life-altering harm proceed — a do-nothing failure. Option (b) is the balanced answer: it protects the colleague through honest, non-specific caution without disclosing the confidential fact, avoids deceiving him, and tackles the real problem by pushing management toward earlier transparency and support. It respects the obligation and the person, and works to shorten the harmful secrecy.
options: Privately tell him about the coming layoff
Give honest general caution without revealing the plan, and press management to communicate and support staff sooner
Reassure him it is safe and encourage the loan
Refuse to discuss it and change the subject
Anonymously leak the restructuring plan to the division

traps: 'He's a friend who deserves to know' tempts you to breach — but selective disclosure is unfair to the other 39 and breaks a legitimate trust.
Actively reassuring/lying to spare discomfort is worse than silence; XAT never credits deception.
Treating the choice as binary (reveal everything vs say nothing) is the trap; the credited path is honest guidance that reveals no confidential fact.

subtopics.name: Fairness, bias & discrimination cases
subtopics.summary: Caselets involving hiring, promotion, appraisal, or treatment where bias — around gender, caste, age, a career gap, background, or a stereotype — is influencing a decision. XAT rewards fixing the PROCESS so the outcome is merit-based and unbiased, rather than either endorsing the biased result or engineering a reverse-biased 'corrective' result.
keyIdeas: The goal is a fair process, not a favoured outcome — strip out the irrelevant/biased criterion and re-judge everyone on job-relevant merit.
Reject both the biased choice AND the over-corrected choice (hiring/promoting someone mainly to fix optics or numbers) — both substitute a non-merit factor for merit.
Address the root: name the biased assumption and have it re-evaluated, rather than rubber-stamping a tainted panel or punishing it without process.
Look for the option that is fair to every candidate simultaneously and would survive scrutiny if all reasoning were made public.

example.q:
Arjun, a hiring manager, is choosing between two strong finalists. The panel narrowly preferred candidate A. Reviewing the notes, Arjun sees that candidate B — a woman returning after a two-year caregiving break — was marked down mainly for the 'gap' and doubts about her 'commitment', while A, with no gap, was given the benefit of the doubt on similar thin evidence. Their job-relevant skills are comparable. What should Arjun do?
(a) Ask the panel to re-evaluate both candidates strictly on job-relevant competencies, explicitly setting aside the employment gap, and decide on that fairer basis.
(b) Go with the panel's preference for A, to respect the team's consensus.
(c) Override the panel and hire B, to correct the bias and improve the team's diversity.
(d) Report the panel to HR for discrimination and discard their assessment entirely.
(e) Hire A, but tell B to reapply once she has 'proven her commitment' after the gap.
example.solution: Correct answer: (a). Endorsing the panel (b) rubber-stamps a biased assessment, and telling B to 'prove commitment' (e) simply repeats the same discriminatory assumption. But flipping to hire B for diversity (c) is a reverse over-correction — it decides on a non-merit factor just as (b) does, only in the other direction. Reporting and discarding the panel (d) is a punitive extreme that skips due process.
