Option (a) is the fair, balanced answer: it removes the biased criterion (the gap), has both candidates judged on genuine job-relevant merit, and would stand up if every reason were made public. It fixes the process rather than manufacturing an outcome, which is the fair and durable choice.
options: Have the panel re-evaluate both on job-relevant merit, setting the employment gap aside
Accept the panel's preference for A to respect consensus
Override and hire B to correct bias and improve diversity
Report the panel to HR and discard their assessment
Hire A and ask B to reapply after she proves her commitment

traps: The 'hire B for diversity' option is tempting as the 'progressive' choice, but engineering an outcome on a non-merit basis is still unfair — XAT wants a fair process, not a corrective result.
Deferring to panel/team consensus is a trap when the consensus itself is built on a biased criterion.
Jumping to punish or report without first correcting the assessment is the harsh extreme, not the balanced answer.

subtopics.name: Pricing & market-entry decisions
subtopics.summary: Caselets where a firm must set a price or decide whether and how to enter a segment/market. XAT frames these as a manager weighing share, margin, brand perception and competitor reaction, and rewards the option that enters sustainably and differentiates rather than the one that simply buys share with the deepest discount.
keyIdeas: Separate the two questions the caselet hides: 'should we enter?' and 'at what price/position?' — the best option usually answers both, not just one.

In a premium or quality-led segment, price is a signal: an aggressive discount can undercut the very positioning the firm is trying to build. Prefer differentiation (sourcing, quality, service) over price as the entry wedge.
Pick the option that protects margin AND perception AND leaves room to react to competitors — reject options that win share today by destroying long-term pricing power.
Use only the numbers and facts the caselet gives; ignore options that assume capacity, cash or data the passage never mentions (e.g., 'acquire the incumbent').

example.q:
Sattva Foods, a profitable mid-sized packaged-foods company, wants to enter the premium organic-snacks segment, currently led by two established brands that command high margins. Buyers in this segment associate price with authenticity and quality. Sattva's marketing head proposes launching at 15% below both incumbents to capture share quickly; the founder worries this will squeeze margins and cheapen the brand. Sattva has a genuine strength: verified local organic farm sourcing that neither incumbent has. Which is the best course of action?
(a) Launch at 15% below both incumbents to grab maximum share fast.
(b) Launch at rough price parity with the incumbents but differentiate on verified local organic sourcing, and scale distribution gradually.
(c) Do not enter — the segment is too competitive.
(d) Launch at a price clearly above both incumbents to signal superiority from day one.
(e) Offer to acquire one of the two incumbent brands instead of launching.
example.solution: Correct: (b). The caselet gives Sattva a real, defensible differentiator (verified local organic sourcing) and tells us buyers read price as a quality signal. Option (b) uses that differentiator, enters at a price that preserves both margin and premium perception, and scales gradually so the firm can watch competitor reaction — the balanced, long-term, resource-realistic choice XLRI expects. (a) is the tempting trap: deep discounting in a premium segment sacrifices margin and actively signals 'cheaper = lower quality', undermining entry; it buys short-term share at long-term cost. (d) launches above proven incumbents with no track record — an unearned premium likely to fail. (c) is the do-nothing extreme, throwing away a genuine advantage. (e) invents an acquisition the caselet never supports (no mention of funds, willingness to sell, or scale) — it ignores 'use only what is stated'.

traps: Assuming 'lower price = more share = right answer'. In premium/quality segments the discount option is usually the plant, because it destroys positioning and margin.
Choosing the flashy 'acquire / merge / raise huge funding' option that the caselet gives no basis for — XAT punishes decisions built on facts not in the passage.
Treating 'don't enter' as automatically the safe, mature answer; it is an extreme too when the firm holds a stated, unused advantage.

subtopics.name: Competitor response & strategy trade-offs
subtopics.summary: Caselets where a rival makes an aggressive move (price cut, cash-burn promotion, copycat launch) and the protagonist firm must respond under constraints. XAT tests whether you can pick a proportionate, resource-aware response instead of either matching the rival blow-for-blow or freezing — the answer must respect the firm's stated runway/capacity and protect its core stakeholders.
keyIdeas: First read the firm's constraint (cash runway, capacity, loyal base). A response the firm cannot sustain is not a strategy — it is a countdown.
Distinguish 'defend the core' from 'win the whole market'. The strong option usually protects loyal customers/employees selectively rather than fighting for every marginal customer.
A cash-burn war started by a better-funded rival is a fight you match only if you can outlast it — otherwise compete on a different axis (reliability, service, loyalty).
Reject options that just deflect the problem (complain to a regulator, do nothing and 'hope') unless the caselet gives concrete grounds — these dodge the actual trade-off.

example.q:
QuickRide, a two-year-old ride-hailing startup with roughly nine months of cash runway, competes in one city. A far better-funded national rival suddenly cuts fares 30% and pays large driver bonuses, openly burning cash to take the city. QuickRide's data shows it has a loyal core of riders and drivers who value its reliability and faster payouts. Matching the rival's fares and bonuses fully would burn QuickRide's runway in about four months. What should QuickRide do?
(a) Match the rival's fare cut and driver bonuses in full to defend market share.
(b) Keep prices and incentives unchanged and wait for the rival to run out of cash.
(c) Protect its loyal core with targeted loyalty benefits and reliable, faster payouts, communicate its value, and respond selectively without matching the full cash burn.
(d) Exit the city and look for a less contested market.
(e) File a predatory-pricing complaint with the regulator and take no commercial action.
example.solution: Correct: (c). The caselet hands you the decisive constraints — limited runway and a stated loyal core that values reliability and fast payouts. (c) competes on that defensible axis, shields the most valuable riders and drivers, and stays within survivable spend: proportionate, stakeholder-inclusive and sustainable. (a) matches a war QuickRide is explicitly told it will lose in about four months — the extreme that trades survival for optics. (b) is the passive extreme: hoping the rival collapses while your own base churns is not a plan and risks losing drivers before the rival blinks. (d) surrenders a market where the firm has a real loyalty advantage — premature retreat. (e) substitutes a regulatory gamble for a business decision and 'takes no commercial action', leaving the core unprotected while the case is pending. Only (c) makes the trade-off the question actually asks about.

traps: Matching the rival 'to defend share' feels decisive but ignores the runway math the caselet deliberately gives you — that is the intended wrong answer.
Pure inaction dressed up as patience ('wait for them to run out of cash') is an extreme; XAT wants an active but proportionate move.

The 'complain to the regulator' option is a deflection: it responds to the rival's tactic instead of protecting the firm's own stakeholders, and the caselet gives no evidence the complaint would succeed.

subtopics.name: Operations & resource allocation
subtopics.summary: Caselets with a hard capacity or resource constraint — one machine, one season of factory capacity, a fixed budget or team — and several competing claims on it. XAT rewards the allocation that honours existing commitments, keeps a realistic buffer, and balances short-term gain against long-term relationships, over the option that maximizes immediate revenue by quietly breaking promises.
keyIdeas: Name the constraint precisely (capacity, time, cash, people) and the claimants on it; the answer is almost always a proportionate split, not 'all to the biggest claimant'.
Standing commitments to loyal/repeat stakeholders carry weight beyond their current revenue — protect them unless the caselet says they are truly disposable.
Look for the option that adds a modest, stated-feasible buffer (overtime, phased timelines, negotiation) instead of betting the whole plan on everything going right.
Any option that keeps a commitment by hiding a broken one (quiet delays, silent downgrades) is disqualified on ethics even if the arithmetic looks good.

example.q:
Meridian Apparel, a garment manufacturer, has limited production capacity for the upcoming season. A large new buyer offers a one-time bulk order that would consume about 80% of Meridian's seasonal capacity at thin margins. Accepting it in full would jeopardize on-time delivery to three loyal repeat clients who together provide Meridian's steady, higher-margin year-round business. Some overtime capacity is available at modest extra cost. What should Meridian's operations head do?
(a) Accept the full bulk order to maximize this season's revenue.
(b) Reject the bulk order entirely to keep existing clients undisturbed.
(c) Accept a partial bulk order sized to preserve committed deliveries to the three loyal clients, negotiate phased timelines with the big buyer, and use the available overtime as a buffer.
(d) Accept the full bulk order and quietly push back the loyal clients' delivery dates.
(e) Accept the full bulk order and outsource the loyal clients' production to a competitor.
example.solution: Correct: (c). The constraint is one season of capacity; the claimants are a large one-off buyer and three loyal, higher-margin repeat clients, and the caselet explicitly offers modest overtime. (c) allocates rationally — it captures part of the new revenue, protects the committed and more valuable long-term relationships, and uses the stated overtime buffer instead of assuming a perfect run. That is the balanced, resource-realistic, stakeholder-inclusive answer. (a) chases thin-margin, one-time revenue at the risk of the steady high-margin base — short-term over long-term. (d) keeps the money by silently breaking delivery promises: dishonest, and the exact ethical trap XAT plants. (e) hands your loyal clients' work — and relationship and quality control — to a competitor, likely losing them for good. (b) is the over-cautious extreme, leaving usable capacity and real revenue on the table when a partial acceptance was clearly feasible.

traps: 'Maximize revenue this season' options ignore that the loyal, higher-margin clients are the long-term business — a classic short-term-over-long-term trap.
Options that keep a promise by secretly breaking another (quiet delays) look efficient but are ruled out on integrity.
Treating outright rejection as the 'safe, relationship-first' choice — it wastes stated spare/overtime capacity, so it is an extreme, not the balance.

subtopics.name: Product / project go–no-go calls
subtopics.summary: Caselets at a decision gate: launch, continue, pause, or kill a product/project after time and money are already spent. XAT tests whether you decide on the evidence in front of you rather than the sunk investment or the team's emotions — the strongest option usually sets a clear, criteria-based conditional go instead of launching a known-flawed product or scrapping a fixable one.
keyIdeas: Money and months already spent are sunk — they cannot justify launching something the evidence says will fail. Decide on expected future outcome, not past cost.

Separate 'the idea is dead' from 'this version has a fixable, identified flaw'. If the caselet names a specific, addressable problem, a gated fix-then-decide beats both a blind launch and an outright kill.
Prefer the option that attaches explicit success criteria or a review checkpoint — that is how XLRI-style decisions stay rational and accountable.
Reject options driven by ego, blame-shifting, or 'we've come too far to stop' — those are the emotional traps the caselet is built to expose.

example.q:
Nucleus Software has spent 18 months and a large budget building a new flagship feature. Pre-launch beta results are clear: users find the core workflow confusing and adoption is weak, and testers point to one specific, well-understood usability flaw. The engineering team is emotionally invested and eager to ship on the announced date; fixing the identified flaw would take an estimated three additional months. Leadership must make a go/no-go call. What is the best decision?
(a) Launch on the announced date — too much time and budget have already gone in to stop now.
(b) Cancel the feature outright and reassign the entire team to other work.
(c) Delay the launch to fix the specific usability flaw the beta identified, set clear adoption criteria for the retest, and decide go/no-go on those results.
(d) Launch quietly to a small segment without fixing the flaw, to save face on the date.
(e) Attribute the weak beta to unrepresentative testers and launch as planned.
example.solution: Correct: (c). The beta gives concrete, actionable evidence and points to one specific, well-understood flaw — meaning the problem is fixable, not fatal. (c) treats the 18 months as sunk, acts on the evidence, targets the named defect, and — crucially — sets explicit adoption criteria for the retest so the final call is rational and accountable. That is the balanced, long-term, evidence-based decision. (a) is the textbook sunk-cost trap: shipping a product you already know confuses users to honour a date will damage the launch and the brand. (e) rationalizes away clear data by blaming the testers — the same trap dressed as denial. (d) tries to protect the date rather than the user and ships the known flaw anyway. (b) over-corrects by killing a feature the caselet says has a specific, addressable issue — discarding a large investment prematurely when a scoped fix is on the table.

traps: 'We've already invested so much, we must launch' is the sunk-cost fallacy XAT loves — invested time never makes a bad launch good.
Dismissing clear negative data as 'the testers were unrepresentative' when the caselet gives no basis for it — decide on the evidence stated, not on convenient excuses.
Jumping straight to 'kill it' when the problem is specifically described as fixable is an over-reaction, not prudent rigor.

subtopics.name: Crisis & reputation management
subtopics.summary: Caselets where a firm discovers a defect, safety risk, or damaging event and must respond, often with reputation and cost pulling against consumer safety. XAT strongly rewards the transparent, proportionate, safety-first response — voluntary disclosure and root-cause action scoped to the actual problem — and penalizes both cover-ups and panicked over-reactions.
keyIdeas: Rank stakeholders: consumer/public safety comes before short-term cost and brand optics. The correct option almost always protects the person at risk first.
Be transparent and proactive — voluntary disclosure and notifying the regulator protect long-term trust far more than the recall costs saved by silence.

Match the response to the scope of the problem: fix/recall the affected batch or unit, not the entire line 'to be safe' — proportionality signals control, over-reaction signals panic and needlessly harms stakeholders.
Always pair the immediate action with a root-cause investigation so the crisis is contained and does not recur.

example.q:
During routine internal testing, packaged-food maker Verdant Foods finds that one production batch of a snack may contain a contaminant slightly above the safe limit. There are no customer complaints yet, and the batch is already on store shelves. A public recall of that batch would be costly and visible. What should Verdant do?
(a) Say nothing publicly, quietly stop shipping the batch, and monitor for complaints.
(b) Issue a transparent voluntary recall of the affected batch, notify the regulator and customers, and launch a root-cause investigation.
(c) Publicly deny any problem to protect the brand, since no complaints have been received.
(d) Immediately recall every product line nationwide, well beyond the affected batch, to look maximally responsible.
(e) Offer steep discounts to clear the remaining affected stock quickly before anyone notices.
example.solution: Correct: (b). A known potential safety risk is already in consumers' hands, so consumer safety is the overriding stakeholder — cost and optics come second. (b) is transparent, proactive and proportionate: it recalls exactly the affected batch, informs the regulator and customers, and investigates the root cause so it will not recur. This protects people now and, by being honest, protects long-term trust far more than the recall's cost. (a) and (c) are cover-ups — staying silent or actively denying a known risk is dishonest and gambles with safety; XAT rejects both outright, and (e) is worse still, deliberately pushing unsafe stock onto customers. (d) errs the other way: recalling every line nationwide when only one batch is implicated is a disproportionate panic response that needlessly harms the business and its other stakeholders and signals loss of control. The balanced, ethical, appropriately scoped action is (b).

traps: 'No complaints yet, so stay quiet' — the absence of complaints never licenses hiding a known safety risk; silence and denial are the dishonest options XAT eliminates first.
Assuming the most drastic response (recall everything nationwide) equals the most responsible one — over-reaction beyond the stated scope is an extreme, not the balance.
Any option that moves affected stock (discounts, quiet clearance) actively harms consumers and is automatically disqualified regardless of the cost it saves.

subtopics.name: Hiring, firing & wrongful termination
subtopics.summary: A people-management caselet where someone is about to be hired, let go, or terminated — usually on incomplete or one-sided information — and you must pick the manager's most appropriate action. XAT loves the 'fire immediately vs. do nothing' trap: the caselet quietly plants mitigating facts (medical leave, a territory reshuffle, a manager who just joined) that a knee-jerk termination would ignore. The expected XLRI answer follows due process — investigate, hear the employee, then act.
keyIdeas: List every fact the caselet states, especially the mitigating ones (leave, restructuring, a new boss, past track record). The 'correct' option is usually the only one that uses ALL of them.
Prefer procedural justice: document performance, hear the employee's side, and give a fair, time-bound improvement plan before any exit. Termination is a last step, not a first reflex.

Reject both extremes — the harsh 'terminate now to send a message' and the soft 'a loyal veteran can never be let go'. XAT rewards the balanced middle that protects the org's standards AND the employee's dignity.
Watch for who is proposing the firing and why (ego, message-sending, recency bias); the right action neutralizes the bias with evidence rather than obeying or defying it outright.

example.q: Rohan, a five-year veteran salesperson who consistently beat his targets, has now missed them for two straight quarters. His regional head — who joined only three months ago — wants him terminated immediately 'to send a message to the team'. HR records show Rohan recently returned from two months of medical leave and that his territory was restructured last quarter. As the HR head, what is the most appropriate action? (a) Endorse the termination, since two quarters of missed targets is objective proof of underperformance. (b) Overrule the regional head outright — a five-year veteran cannot be let go. (c) Ask the regional head to first hold a documented performance review with Rohan that weighs his medical leave and the territory change, and to agree a time-bound improvement plan before termination is even considered. (d) Quietly transfer Rohan to another region so the regional head is placated and Rohan keeps a job. (e) Issue Rohan a formal warning letter and freeze any decision on him for a year.
example.solution: Correct: (c). It is the only option that uses the mitigating facts the caselet supplies — the medical leave and the territory restructuring both plausibly explain the dip — and it follows fair process: diagnose with a documented review, then give a time-bound improvement plan before any exit. This protects the firm's performance standards and Rohan's dignity at once. (a) is the knee-jerk-fire trap: it treats the numbers as 'objective' while ignoring stated context and rewards a new manager's urge to make an example of someone. (b) is the opposite extreme, defending the veteran on loyalty alone with no diagnosis. (d) sidesteps the real question and is an arbitrary, unfair fix. (e) skips diagnosis and adds an arbitrary year-long freeze — neither fair to Rohan nor useful to the team.

traps: The option that calls raw numbers 'objective evidence' and fires at once — it deliberately omits the mitigating facts the caselet handed you.
The equal-and-opposite trap of refusing any action out of loyalty or seniority; XLRI wants fairness, not immunity.
A 'clever' sidestep (transfer, indefinite hold) that avoids confronting the actual performance question.

subtopics.name: Team conflict & toxic employees
subtopics.summary: A caselet built around interpersonal friction — a high performer who demoralizes the team, a clash between two members, or a manager caught between output and morale. XAT's twist is the 'indispensable but toxic star': the person delivers critical work, so both keeping them unchanged and firing them on the spot are wrong. The XLRI-approved answer confronts the behaviour directly with specific feedback and clear consequences while trying to retain the talent and protect the team.
keyIdeas: Separate the two facts the caselet gives you: strong output AND documented harmful behaviour. The right option addresses the behaviour without pretending the output does not matter.
Look for direct, specific, private feedback tied to the actual incidents, plus clear consequences and measurable expectations — that is procedural fairness applied to conduct.
Never solve conflict by punishing the people who raised it (transferring the complainants) or by pushing the problem into the future (just 'keep documenting').

Balance three stakeholders — the individual, the team's morale, and the project's delivery — and pick the action that serves all three over time, not the one that only rescues this quarter's deadline.

example.q: Meera is the most productive engineer on a ten-person team and single-handedly owns a critical module. But three teammates have complained that she publicly ridicules others' code and takes sole credit in reviews, and two juniors have asked to transfer citing her behaviour. The team lead values her output and fears the project will slip if she leaves. What should the team lead most appropriately do? (a) Retain Meera as she is — her output is indispensable and the juniors must toughen up. (b) Remove Meera from the team at once to protect morale. (c) Give Meera specific, private feedback on the documented incidents, make clear that the behaviour has consequences regardless of her output, set measurable conduct expectations, and pair her constructively with the juniors. (d) Reassign the two complaining juniors to other teams to defuse the tension. (e) Ask the juniors to document every future incident and escalate only if it recurs.
example.solution: Correct: (c). It confronts the real problem — the conduct — with specific, documented feedback and unambiguous consequences, while still giving a valuable engineer a fair chance to correct course and keeping the module on track. That is the balanced, procedurally just response XLRI expects. (a) tolerates toxicity and blames the victims, which corrodes team morale and eventually costs more than Meera's output is worth. (b) is the knee-jerk removal that throws away critical talent without ever giving her a chance to change. (d) punishes the people who were harmed and rewards the behaviour. (e) is passive: it delays action, shifts the burden onto the juniors, and leaves the harm ongoing.

traps: The 'she's too valuable to touch' option that tells the juniors to toughen up — it sacrifices the team to protect one star.
The instant-removal option that treats firing as the only way to protect morale, ignoring that feedback plus consequences might fix it.
Any option that relocates or burdens the complainants instead of addressing the toxic behaviour itself.

subtopics.name: Appraisals, promotions & disputes
subtopics.summary: A caselet where an appraisal rating, a promotion, or a reward is contested — one person is passed over, two people compete for one slot, or an employee alleges their quiet, non-glamorous work is undervalued. XAT tests whether you defend a fair, transparent, criteria-based process rather than reflexively siding with revenue, seniority, or the loudest claim. The XLRI answer re-examines the process against the stated criteria and corrects it only if it was applied inconsistently.
keyIdeas: Anchor on the agreed appraisal criteria, not on gut sympathies. Ask: was the process applied consistently and were all contributions actually recorded and weighted?
Treat a grievance as a signal to audit the process, not as a demand to reverse the outcome. Investigate first; correct only if the process was genuinely flawed or biased.
Reject rewards based purely on visibility/revenue OR purely on loyalty/seniority — both are single-factor shortcuts. Merit judged against the full, stated criteria is the XLRI standard.
Communicate the reasoning transparently to everyone affected; procedural justice is as much about the explanation as the decision.

example.q: In the annual appraisals, Anil — a diligent employee whose back-office work quietly prevented several compliance failures — gets a 'meets expectations' rating and is passed over for promotion in favour of Sanjay, a more recent joiner who led a high-visibility client win. Anil formally appeals, arguing that steady, preventive work is treated as 'invisible' next to flashy front-office wins. As the department head hearing the appeal, what should you do? (a) Uphold the original decision — client wins drive revenue and must outrank back-office work. (b) Reverse it and promote Anil, since loyalty and steady service should count most. (c) Re-examine both cases against the stated appraisal criteria, verify whether Anil's compliance contributions were actually recorded and weighted, correct the rating and decision if the criteria were applied inconsistently, and explain the reasoning to both employees. (d) Promote both Anil and Sanjay to avoid the conflict. (e) Tell Anil to make his work more visible next year and move on.
example.solution: Correct: (c). Anil's appeal is a procedural concern — that a whole category of valuable work may not have been captured — so the fair response is to audit the process against the agreed criteria and fix it only if it was applied unevenly, then communicate transparently. This upholds merit while taking the grievance seriously. (a) dismisses a legitimate process question and hard-codes a revenue-only bias. (b) overcorrects into a loyalty/seniority bias, which is just as arbitrary as the one being complained about. (d) buys peace by abandoning any standard and is unsustainable. (e) brushes off the grievance without ever checking whether the process was fair.

traps: The option that ranks revenue-generating visibility above everything and calls the case closed — it ignores that the complaint is about the process, not the person.
The reverse trap of promoting on loyalty/seniority, which swaps one bias for another.
The 'promote both / keep everyone happy' escape that dissolves the standard instead of applying it.

subtopics.name: Diversity, inclusion & workplace equity
subtopics.summary: A caselet posing a genuine diversity or fairness goal against a specific merit-based decision — a leader ordering a diversity hire 'regardless of the shortlist', an inclusion incident, or unequal treatment. XAT frames it so both blind tokenism and ignoring diversity are wrong. The XLRI answer honours the diversity objective by fixing the system — pipeline, sourcing, and biased criteria — rather than forcing an unfair one-off, keeping the individual selection merit-based.
keyIdeas: Distinguish the legitimate goal (a more diverse, inclusive org) from an illegitimate method (overriding merit for one hire). Endorse the goal, redirect the method.
Attack the root cause — a narrow pipeline, biased job criteria, a weak funnel — so more qualified diverse candidates reach the shortlist. That is durable equity, not a cosmetic fix.
Avoid both extremes: a quota that undermines the hire's own credibility (tokenism) and a 'pure score' stance that dismisses a real, stated organizational problem.
Prefer the long-term systemic fix over the short-term optic, and keep the specific selection fair to everyone already on the shortlist.

example.q: A tech firm's entire leadership team is male. The CEO, wanting to improve gender diversity, tells HR to hire a woman for the next senior opening 'regardless of the shortlist'. The current shortlist — drawn from an open, agreed process — has three strong male candidates and one woman who is competent but ranks third on the criteria. What is the most appropriate response for the HR head? (a) Follow the CEO's instruction and hire the woman candidate to meet the diversity goal. (b) Ignore the diversity concern and hire the top-ranked candidate strictly by score. (c) Explain that overriding merit for this hire risks tokenism and unfairness, and instead propose widening the sourcing pipeline, auditing the job criteria for hidden bias, and strengthening the funnel so more qualified women reach future shortlists — while keeping this particular selection merit-based. (d) Split the role into two positions so both a man and the woman can be hired. (e) Delay the hire indefinitely until a woman naturally tops the shortlist.
example.solution: Correct: (c). It affirms the CEO's legitimate diversity goal but rejects the unfair method, redirecting effort to the systemic causes — sourcing, biased criteria, a thin pipeline — that actually produce a male-only shortlist. That yields durable equity without shortchanging the candidates already competing fairly, and it spares the eventual woman hire the stigma of being a token. (a) is tokenism: it is unfair to the shortlist and quietly undermines the credibility of the very hire it intends to help. (b) ignores a real, explicitly stated organizational problem. (d) is a wasteful dodge that manufactures a role rather than confronting the issue. (e) is do-nothing dressed as patience — an indefinite delay is not a plan.

traps: The 'just comply with the boss and hire for the quota' option — it looks decisive and pro-diversity but is tokenism and is unfair to everyone, including the hire.
The equal-and-opposite 'merit only, diversity is not my problem' option that dismisses a legitimate, stated goal.
Gimmick fixes (split the role, wait indefinitely) that avoid the systemic pipeline problem the caselet is really about.

subtopics.name: Financial trade-offs (cost vs principle)
subtopics.summary: A caselet gives a real rupee saving that comes bundled with an ethical, safety or reputational compromise, and asks what a manager should do. XLRI wants the option that respects the principle without ignoring the cost pressure, so the answer is judgment-led even though numbers are on the page.
keyIdeas: Separate the two axes: how big is the money saved, and what exactly is compromised to save it (safety, honesty, a stated commitment, a stakeholder).
Reject any option that trades a non-negotiable (patient/customer safety, legality, honesty) for a short-term saving, however large the number looks.
Prefer the balanced option that protects the principle AND still addresses cost — negotiate, audit, phase in — over both the pure-profit choice and the passive do-nothing choice.
Use only what the caselet states; do not assume the cheaper supplier is fine or the costlier one is safe without evidence.

example.q: Arogya, a hospital pharmacy chain, currently buys a life-saving generic from certified Supplier Z at Rs 20 per strip (volume 2,00,000 strips a month). Supplier X offers the same drug at Rs 18 (10% cheaper, a saving of Rs 4 lakh a month) but has twice been penalised by the drug regulator for adulteration. Supplier Y is certified but charges Rs 22. What should the procurement head do? (a) Switch to X to capture the Rs 4 lakh monthly saving; the penalties are old news. (b) Switch to Y, since the highest price must mean the safest drug. (c) Reject X on safety grounds, keep a certified supply, and negotiate a better rate with Z while inviting X to clear an independent quality audit before it is ever reconsidered. (d) Do nothing; procurement tinkering is not worth the effort. (e) Quietly mix X and Z stock and see whether patients notice any difference.
example.solution: The saving is real (Rs 4 lakh a month) but it is bought by risking patient safety with a repeat-adulteration supplier — a non-negotiable, so (a) and the reckless (e) are out. (b) overpays on the false assumption that price equals safety. (d) ducks the genuine cost pressure. (c) is the balanced, principled answer: it protects safety by keeping a certified supply, yet still attacks cost by negotiating with Z, and it leaves a fair, evidence-based door open for X (an independent audit) rather than banning it emotionally. Answer: (c).

traps: Being anchored by the big saving (Rs 4 lakh) and treating a safety/ethics red flag as a mere 'risk' to price in.
Assuming the most expensive option is automatically the safe or ethical one.
Picking the do-nothing option and calling it 'principled' when a balanced fix (negotiate/audit) was available.

subtopics.name: Data & table-driven decisions
subtopics.summary: A small table of numbers (costs, margins, defect rates, times) is given and the decision is objective — it lives in the arithmetic. XAT frames it managerially, but on these analytical items you must actually compute the right per-unit or per-constraint figure rather than eyeball the headline number.
formulas: Contribution per unit of the scarce resource = contribution per unit / units of that resource used per unit
When one resource is the bottleneck, rank products by contribution per unit of that resource (highest first), not by contribution per unit
keyIdeas: Identify what is actually scarce (machine hours, budget, floor space) and express every option per unit of that scarce thing.
Do the small computation for each row before comparing; the winner on a per-unit basis is often the loser on a per-bottleneck basis.
Only bring in a criterion the caselet gives you; if the data ties, say 'insufficient information' rather than inventing a tie-breaker.

example.q: A plant makes three products, and machine time is the binding constraint (demand for all three exceeds available hours). Per unit: Product A earns Rs 300 contribution and needs 3 machine-hours; Product B earns Rs 240 and needs 2 hours; Product C earns Rs 180 and needs 1 hour. To maximise total profit from the limited machine time, which product should the plant prioritise? (a) A, it has the highest contribution per unit. (b) B. (c) C. (d) Make equal quantities of all three. (e) Cannot be determined from the data.
example.solution: The scarce resource is machine-hours, so compute contribution per machine-hour: A = 300/3 = Rs 100/hr, B = 240/2 = Rs 120/hr, C = 180/1 = Rs 180/hr. C yields the most profit for every scarce hour, so filling capacity with C first maximises total profit. Answer: (c). Prioritising A on its Rs 300 headline contribution actually gives the least profit per bottleneck hour.

traps: Choosing the product with the highest contribution per unit (A) instead of per bottleneck hour (C).
Ignoring which resource is actually scarce, so the per-unit ranking is computed on the wrong base.
Reaching for 'cannot be determined' when the table in fact fixes the answer.

subtopics.name: Bias removal & rational judgment
subtopics.summary: The caselet is engineered to trigger a cognitive bias — most often the sunk-cost fallacy, but also anchoring or base-rate neglect — and the rational answer is the one that strips the bias out. On these items the correct choice is objective once you look only at forward-looking cash flows.
formulas: Continue only if (value gained by finishing) > (remaining cost to finish); money already spent is a sunk cost and is excluded from the decision
keyIdeas: Name the bias the caselet is baiting (sunk cost, anchor, confirmation) so you can consciously discount it.
Compare only future costs against future benefits; treat everything already spent as gone and irrelevant.
Beware options that quote the money-already-spent as a reason to persist — that phrasing is the tell of the sunk-cost trap.

example.q: A firm has already spent Rs 12 crore developing a product. Completing it will cost a further Rs 5 crore, after which it can be sold for Rs 4 crore. If the firm abandons the project now, it can salvage Rs 2 crore. What is the rational decision? (a) Continue — walking away wastes the Rs 12 crore already invested. (b) Abandon now and take the Rs 2 crore salvage. (c) Continue, because the Rs 4 crore sale value exceeds the Rs 2 crore salvage. (d) Invest Rs 2.5 crore and stop halfway. (e) Continue, since more than half the money is already spent.
example.solution: The Rs 12 crore is a sunk cost and must be ignored. Going forward, finishing means paying Rs 5 crore to receive Rs 4 crore — a net loss of Rs 1 crore. Abandoning yields +Rs 2 crore now. So abandoning beats finishing by Rs 3 crore. (c) is wrong because getting the Rs 4 crore still costs Rs 5 crore; (d) produces nothing sellable; (a) and (e) are pure sunk-cost fallacy. Answer: (b).

traps: Choosing to continue because 'we have already invested so much' — the textbook sunk-cost fallacy.
Comparing the completion value (Rs 4 cr) to salvage (Rs 2 cr) while forgetting the Rs 5 cr it costs to reach that value.
Anchoring on the largest number on the page (Rs 12 cr) even though it no longer affects any future outcome.

subtopics.name: Budgeting & investment calls
subtopics.summary: Given a fixed budget and a menu of projects with their outlays and returns, choose the subset that maximises return within the constraint. The answer is objective — you must check feasibility against the budget and compare total returns, not just pick the flashiest single ROI.
formulas: ROI = annual net return / investment x 100
Payback period = investment / annual net cash flow
Under a hard budget cap, maximise total return across FEASIBLE subsets, not the return of any one project
keyIdeas: List the feasible combinations that fit the budget, then compare their total returns; the best single-ROI project is often not part of the best portfolio.
Check the budget constraint before celebrating a high-return pair — the top combined return may simply be unaffordable.
Leaving budget idle (funding too little) is usually inferior to using the full cap if a feasible fuller combination returns more.

example.q: A firm has a Rs 100 lakh capital budget and three independent, indivisible projects. P: invest Rs 60 lakh, annual net return Rs 18 lakh. Q: invest Rs 40 lakh, return Rs 14 lakh. R: invest Rs 50 lakh, return Rs 15 lakh. Which choice maximises annual return within the budget? (a) Fund only Q, which has the highest ROI (35%). (b) Fund P and R for the largest combined return of Rs 33 lakh. (c) Fund P and Q. (d) Fund Q and R to keep Rs 10 lakh in reserve. (e) Fund all three projects.
example.solution: ROIs are P 30%, Q 35%, R 30%. Test feasible subsets against the Rs 100 lakh cap: P+Q costs 60+40 = 100 (fits) and returns 18+14 = Rs 32 lakh; Q+R costs 90 and returns 29; P+R costs 110 and R alone... P+R (b) returns 33 but costs Rs 110 lakh, over budget, so it is infeasible; all three (e) costs 150, infeasible. Funding only Q (a) uses just Rs 40 lakh and returns Rs 14 lakh, leaving the budget idle. The best feasible option is P+Q, using the full budget for Rs 32 lakh. Answer: (c).

traps: Picking the highest-ROI single project (Q) and leaving most of the budget unused.
Being lured by the biggest combined return (P+R, Rs 33 lakh) without checking it costs Rs 110 lakh and breaches the budget.
Treating a cash reserve as a virtue (Q+R) when a fully-invested feasible combination returns more.

subtopics.name: Percentages
subtopics.summary: Converting between fractions, ratios and percentages and handling percentage change. On XAT it rarely appears as a bare 'find x%' question; instead it is the language of profit-loss, data-interpretation caselets, elections, population growth and income problems, so fluency here pays off across the whole QA-DI section.
formulas: x% of y = (x/100)·y; a as a percent of b = (a/b)×100%
Percentage change = (new − old)/old × 100
Successive changes of a% then b%: net% = a + b + (a·b)/100 (use signs; a decrease is negative)
If A is x% more than B, then B is [x/(100+x)]×100% less than A
If A is x% less than B, then B is [x/(100−x)]×100% more than A
keyIdeas: Turn every percent into a multiplier and chain them: +25% then −20% is 1.25×0.80 = 1.00, i.e. no net change. This kills successive-change problems in one line.
For 'A is x% more than B, so B is ? % less than A', switch the base — the two percentages are never equal (e.g. 25% more ⇒ 20% less).
Assume the base is 100 (or the LCM of the denominators) when no number is given; it converts percentages straight into countable units.

example.q: A number is first increased by 20% and then the result is decreased by 20%. The final value is 40 less than the original number. What was the original number? (a) 800  (b) 900  (c) 1000  (d) 1100  (e) 1200
example.solution: Net multiplier = 1.20 × 0.80 = 0.96, a net decrease of 4%. This 4% equals the given drop of 40, so 0.04·N = 40 ⇒ N = 1000. (Check: 1000→1200→960, which is indeed 40 less than 1000.) Answer: (c) 1000.

traps: Assuming a +20% then −20% returns you to the start — it always leaves a net loss of (20²/100)=4%.
Keeping the same base for the reverse comparison: 'x% more' and the corresponding 'x% less' use different bases and differ.

subtopics.name: Ratio & proportion
subtopics.summary: Splitting quantities in a given ratio, combining chained ratios, and solving proportions where a quantity is added to or removed from each term. XAT loves the 'incomes in one ratio, expenditures in another, equal savings' template and ratio-of-mixtures wording, often as a two-equation setup disguised in words.
formulas: a:b = c:d ⟺ ad = bc (product of extremes = product of means)
Dividing N in ratio a:b:c → shares are N·a/(a+b+c), N·b/(a+b+c), N·c/(a+b+c)
Componendo–dividendo: if a/b = c/d then (a+b)/(a−b) = (c+d)/(c−d)
To merge a:b and b:c into a:b:c, scale so the common term b matches (take LCM of the two b-values)
Compare two ratios a/b and c/d by cross-multiplying: a/b > c/d ⟺ ad > bc
keyIdeas: Introduce a single scaling constant: write the terms as 5x and 4x, not 5 and 4, so every condition becomes a linear equation in x.
When two independent ratios (e.g. income and expenditure) meet a shared condition (equal savings), use two constants x and y and solve the pair simultaneously.
Keep ratio parts as integers by scaling out fractions before dividing a total — it prevents rounding errors when options are close.

example.q: The incomes of A and B are in the ratio 5 : 4 and their expenditures are in the ratio 3 : 2. If each of them saves ₹1600 at the end of the month, what is A's monthly income? (a) ₹3200  (b) ₹3600  (c) ₹4000  (d) ₹4400  (e) ₹4800
example.solution: Let incomes be 5x and 4x, expenditures 3y and 2y. Savings: 5x − 3y = 1600 and 4x − 2y = 1600. The second gives 2x − y = 800 ⇒ y = 2x − 800. Substituting: 5x − 3(2x − 800) = 1600 ⇒ −x + 2400 = 1600 ⇒ x = 800. A's income = 5x = ₹4000. (Check: A saves 4000 − 2400 = 1600; B saves 3200 − 1600 = 1600.) Answer: (c) ₹4000.

traps: Treating the two ratios (income and expenditure) as if they shared the same constant — they need separate variables x and y.
Adding a constant to each term does not preserve the ratio: (a+k):(b+k) ≠ a:b, so you must set up an equation, not scale.

subtopics.name: Averages, mixtures & alligation
subtopics.summary: Simple and weighted averages, replacement of one mixture component with another, and the alligation rule for blending two grades to hit a target price or concentration. XAT often hides alligation inside a profit-loss shell ('mix teas, sell at a gain of x%') so you must first back out the required mean value, then alligate.
formulas: Average = (sum of all quantities)/(number of quantities)
Weighted average of groups: (n₁·A₁ + n₂·A₂)/(n₁ + n₂)
Alligation: (quantity of cheaper)/(quantity of dearer) = (Dearer − Mean)/(Mean − Cheaper)
Repeated replacement: after removing x and topping up n times from volume V, pure liquid left = V·(1 − x/V)^n
Adding pure quantity to change a ratio: track the unchanged component (its amount stays fixed) to set up one equation
keyIdeas: Draw the alligation cross: put the two costs on top corners, the mean in the middle, and read the cross-differences as the required ratio (cheaper : dearer).
If a selling price and profit% are given, first convert to the cost of the mixture (CP = SP ÷ (1+profit/100)) — that CP is the 'mean' you alligate to.
In add-water / add-milk problems, the quantity you do NOT add stays constant; anchor your equation on that fixed quantity.

example.q: In what ratio must a grocer mix two varieties of tea costing ₹60/kg and ₹65/kg so that by selling the mixture at ₹68.20/kg he makes a profit of 10%? (a) 2 : 3  (b) 3 : 2  (c) 5 : 6  (d) 6 : 5  (e) 1 : 1
example.solution: A 10% profit means cost of the mixture = 68.20 ÷ 1.10 = ₹62/kg. Alligation about the mean 62: cheaper (60) : dearer (65) = (65 − 62) : (62 − 60) = 3 : 2. Answer: (b) 3 : 2.

traps: Alligating directly to the selling price (68.20) instead of first stripping the 10% profit to get the true mean cost (62).
Reversing the cross: the difference nearest the DEARER price gives the share of the CHEAPER item, not the dearer one.

subtopics.name: Profit, loss & discount
subtopics.summary: Cost price, marked price, discount and profit/loss percentages, and the classic 'two items sold at the same price' and 'markup-then-discount' setups. XAT 2025 was arithmetic-heavy and this is a staple; watch for the trap where equal % gain and loss on two equal selling prices produce a net loss, not break-even.
formulas: Profit% = (SP − CP)/CP × 100; Loss% = (CP − SP)/CP × 100
SP = CP × (100 + Profit%)/100; CP = SP × 100/(100 + Profit%)
With markup m% and discount d% on marked price: net profit% = m − d − (m·d)/100
Two articles sold at the SAME price, one at +x% and one at −x%: always a net LOSS of (x²/100)%
Discount% = (MP − SP)/MP × 100
keyIdeas: Fix CP = 100 (or 100 per unit) and just push it through the multipliers: markup ×(1+m/100), discount ×(1−d/100). The final number minus 100 is the profit%.
For 'same SP, equal +x% and −x%' problems, skip the algebra — the answer is a fixed (x/10)% loss.
Always confirm which base a percentage sits on: profit% is on CP, discount% is on MP. Mixing the two bases is the single biggest error here.

example.q: A man sells two articles for ₹1000 each. On one he gains 25% and on the other he loses 25%. Over the two transactions together, his result is: (a) no profit, no loss  (b) 6.25% loss  (c) 6.25% gain  (d) 5% loss  (e) 4% gain
example.solution: CP of the gainer = 1000/1.25 = ₹800; CP of the loser = 1000/0.75 = ₹1333.33. Total CP = ₹2133.33, total SP = ₹2000, so loss = 133.33/2133.33 = 6.25%. (Matches the shortcut: equal ±25% at equal SP gives (25²/100)% = 6.25% loss.) Answer: (b) 6.25% loss.

traps: Concluding 'gain 25% and lose 25% cancel out' — equal % on the same selling price always nets a loss of x²/100 %.
Applying the discount to the cost price instead of the marked price, or adding profit% and discount% directly without the interaction term m·d/100.

subtopics.name: Simple & compound interest
subtopics.summary: Simple vs. compound interest, converting between successive-year amounts to extract the rate, and the CI−SI difference results. XAT keeps these numeric and often gives two consecutive amounts (or the 2-year CI−SI gap) and asks for the principal or rate, so know the difference formulas cold.
formulas: Simple interest: SI = P·R·T/100; Amount = P + SI
Compound interest: Amount A = P(1 + R/100)^n; CI = A − P
Under CI, ratio of amounts in consecutive years = (1 + R/100): A₍ₙ₊₁₎ / Aₙ = 1 + R/100
CI − SI for 2 years = P(R/100)²
CI − SI for 3 years = P(R/100)²·(3 + R/100)
keyIdeas: Given amounts for two consecutive years, divide the later by the earlier — the quotient is (1 + R/100), which hands you the rate instantly.
The extra interest CI earns over SI in year 2 is just 'interest on the first year's interest' — that intuition reproduces P(R/100)² without memorising.
For half-yearly compounding, halve the rate and double the number of periods; for quarterly, quarter the rate and quadruple the periods.

example.q: A sum of money invested at compound interest amounts to ₹9680 in 2 years and ₹10648 in 3 years. What is the principal? (a) ₹7500  (b) ₹8000  (c) ₹8500  (d) ₹8800  (e) ₹9000
example.solution: The interest in the 3rd year is on the 2-year amount, so 10648/9680 = 1.10, giving R = 10%. Then P(1.10)² = 9680 ⇒ P = 9680/1.21 = ₹8000. Answer: (b) ₹8000.

traps: Using P(1+R/100)^n with the wrong number of years — 'amount in 2 years' means n = 2, not 3.
Confusing the 2-year and 3-year CI−SI formulas: the 3-year gap carries the extra (3 + R/100) factor, not just P(R/100)².

subtopics.name: Time, speed & distance
subtopics.summary: Uniform-motion basics, unit conversion, relative speed for trains/meetings, average speed over equal distances, and boats-and-streams. On XAT expect trains crossing platforms or one another, and the average-speed trap where the answer is the harmonic mean, not the arithmetic mean, of the two speeds.
formulas: Speed = Distance/Time; 1 km/h = 5/18 m/s and 1 m/s = 18/5 km/h
Train of length L crossing a platform of length P: time = (L + P)/speed
Average speed over equal distances at speeds a and b = 2ab/(a + b) (harmonic mean)
Relative speed: opposite directions = a + b; same direction = a − b
Boats: downstream = (b + s), upstream = (b − s); boat speed b = (down + up)/2, stream s = (down − up)/2
keyIdeas: Lock your units first: convert every km/h to m/s (×5/18) when lengths are in metres and times in seconds — most train errors are unit slips.
When a body crosses a platform or another train, the distance covered is the SUM of both lengths; when it passes a pole, only its own length.
For a there-and-back trip at two speeds, use 2ab/(a+b); it is always less than the simple average because more time is spent at the slower speed.

example.q: A train 200 m long crosses a platform 300 m long in 25 seconds. What is the speed of the train? (a) 60 km/h  (b) 66 km/h  (c) 72 km/h  (d) 75 km/h  (e) 80 km/h
example.solution: Distance covered = train + platform = 200 + 300 = 500 m, in 25 s. Speed = 500/25 = 20 m/s = 20 × 18/5 = 72 km/h. Answer: (c) 72 km/h.

traps: Taking average speed of a round trip as the arithmetic mean (e.g. 50 for 40 & 60) instead of the harmonic mean 2ab/(a+b) = 48.
Forgetting to add the platform length, or leaving speed in m/s when the options are in km/h (or vice-versa).

subtopics.name: Time & work
subtopics.summary: Rate-based work sharing, partial work with people joining or leaving mid-way, and pipes-and-cisterns (which are just work problems with a leak as negative work). XAT phrases these as multi-stage caselets ('they work together k days, then A leaves'), so tracking the fraction of work done at each stage is the whole game.
formulas: If A finishes a job in 'a' days, A's one-day work = 1/a
Combined rate = sum of individual rates; time together = 1/(sum of rates)
Work done = rate × time; remaining work = 1 − (work already done)
Take total work = LCM of the given days so each person's daily work is a whole number of units
Men-days-hours: (M₁·D₁·H₁)/W₁ = (M₂·D₂·H₂)/W₂
keyIdeas: Set total work to the LCM of the individual times so rates become integer 'units per day' — no fractions, fewer slips.
For staged problems, compute the fraction of work completed in the joint phase first, subtract from 1, then divide the remainder by the remaining worker's rate.
A leaking pipe or an outlet is negative work; add it as a negative rate to the combined rate.

example.q: A can do a piece of work in 12 days and B in 18 days. They work together for 4 days, after which A leaves. In how many more days will B finish the remaining work? (a) 6  (b) 7  (c) 8  (d) 9  (e) 10
example.solution: Rates: A = 1/12, B = 1/18, together = 3/36 + 2/36 = 5/36 per day. In 4 days they complete 4 × 5/36 = 20/36 = 5/9 of the work. Remaining = 4/9. B alone finishes it in (4/9) ÷ (1/18) = (4/9) × 18 = 8 days. Answer: (c) 8.

traps: Adding the days (12 + 18) or averaging them instead of adding the rates (1/12 + 1/18).
Dividing the remaining work by the COMBINED rate after A has already left — it must be divided by B's rate alone.

subtopics.name: Partnership
subtopics.summary: Sharing profit in proportion to each partner's capital weighted by the time it stayed invested. XAT tests this less often than the core arithmetic topics, but when it appears it usually stages partners joining or withdrawing at different months, so the profit ratio is capital × time, not capital alone.
formulas: Profit share ∝ (capital × time invested): share ratio = C_A·t_A : C_B·t_B : …
Equal-time (simple) partnership: share ∝ capital only
Each partner's profit = Total profit × (that partner's capital·time)/(sum of all capital·time)
Working partner's salary/commission is deducted from total profit first, then the remainder is split by capital·time
keyIdeas: Compute 'capital-months' (money × number of months invested) for each partner, reduce that ratio to its simplest form, then divide the profit by the total parts.
Someone who joins late or withdraws early gets fewer months — always use the actual months invested, not 12.
If a partner draws a salary for managing the business, subtract it before splitting the balance by capital-months.

example.q: A starts a business with ₹20,000. Five months later B joins with ₹30,000. At the end of the year the total profit is ₹45,000. What is B's share of the profit? (a) ₹18,000  (b) ₹20,000  (c) ₹21,000  (d) ₹22,500  (e) ₹24,000
example.solution: Capital-months: A = 20,000 × 12 = 240,000; B joins after 5 months, so B invests for 7 months = 30,000 × 7 = 210,000. Ratio A : B = 240,000 : 210,000 = 8 : 7, total 15 parts. B's share = 45,000 × 7/15 = ₹21,000. Answer: (c) ₹21,000.

traps: Splitting profit by capital alone (20,000 : 30,000 = 2 : 3) and ignoring that B was invested for only 7 months.
Counting B's months as 5 (the month he joined) instead of the 7 months he was actually invested until year-end.

subtopics.name: Linear & quadratic equations
subtopics.summary: Solving single/simultaneous linear equations and quadratics, and reasoning about the roots of a quadratic without solving it. XAT rarely asks a plain 'find the roots'; it wraps the idea in a condition (equal roots, roots in a given ratio, a symmetric function of the roots) so you must use the sum/product relations and the discriminant.
formulas: Quadratic ax^2 + bx + c = 0 (a != 0): roots x = [-b ± √(b^2 - 4ac)] / (2a)
Sum of roots α + β = -b/a; Product of roots αβ = c/a
Discriminant D = b^2 - 4ac: D > 0 → two distinct real roots, D = 0 → equal (repeated) roots, D < 0 → complex conjugate roots
Reconstruct a quadratic from its roots: x^2 - (α + β)x + αβ = 0
Useful symmetric identities: α^2 + β^2 = (α + β)^2 - 2αβ; α^3 + β^3 = (α + β)^3 - 3αβ(α + β); 1/α + 1/β = (α + β)/αβ
keyIdeas: When a condition is on the roots (equal, real, in ratio m:n, one root k times the other), translate it into an equation using sum = -b/a, product = c/a, or D = 0 — do NOT try to find the roots explicitly.
Any symmetric expression in the roots (α^2+β^2, α^3+β^3, 1/α+1/β) can be written in terms of (α+β) and αβ, so you never need the individual roots.
'Equal roots' / 'exactly one solution' / 'the line is tangent' all mean the same thing: set the discriminant to 0.

example.q:
The equation x^2 - (k + 2)x + (k + 5) = 0 has equal roots. What is the sum of all possible values of k?
(a) -8  (b) -4  (c) 0  (d) 4  (e) 8
example.solution: Equal roots means the discriminant is 0. Here b = -(k+2) and c = (k+5), so D = (k+2)^2 - 4(k+5) = 0. Expand: k^2 + 4k + 4 - 4k - 20 = 0 → k^2 - 16 = 0 → k = 4 or k = -4. The sum of all possible k values is 4 + (-4) = 0. Answer: (c) 0.

traps: Confusing the coefficient signs: with x^2 - (k+2)x + (k+5), the sum of roots is +(k+2), not -(k+2). Read a, b, c off the standard form carefully.
Forgetting that a 'quadratic' requires a != 0 — if a problem has a parameter multiplying x^2, the case a = 0 (which makes it linear) is a separate solution branch that is easy to miss.

subtopics.name: Inequalities & modulus
subtopics.summary: Solving linear/quadratic and absolute-value inequalities and counting integer solutions. XAT favours modulus expressions with two or more |·| terms (interpret them as distances on the number line) and 'how many integers satisfy' phrasing, which turns an inequality into a counting problem.
formulas: |x| < a ⟺ -a < x < a; |x| > a ⟺ x < -a or x > a (for a > 0)
|x - a| is the distance between x and a on the number line
Triangle inequality: |a + b| ≤ |a| + |b|; also | |a| - |b| | ≤ |a - b|
For a quadratic inequality, factor and use the sign-scheme (wavy-curve) method between the roots
AM ≥ GM (for non-negatives): (a + b)/2 ≥ √(ab), with equality iff a = b
keyIdeas: Read |x - p| + |x - q| as 'distance from p plus distance from q'; its minimum equals |p - q| and is achieved for every x between p and q — this kills most two-modulus problems instantly.
To solve |x - p| + |x - q| ≤ k, only the two outer linear pieces matter: solve the right piece and the left piece to get the interval [low, high], then count integers inside.
Never multiply an inequality by a variable of unknown sign; multiply only by known-positive quantities, or bring everything to one side and analyse signs.

example.q:
How many integer values of x satisfy |x - 3| + |x + 2| ≤ 8?
(a) 6  (b) 7  (c) 8  (d) 9  (e) 10
example.solution: The left side is the sum of distances of x from 3 and from -2, whose minimum is the gap between them, 5, attained for all x in [-2, 3] (so it is ≤ 8 there). For x > 3 the sum is (x-3)+(x+2) = 2x - 1 ≤ 8 → x ≤ 4.5. For x < -2 the sum is (3-x)+(-x-2) = 1 - 2x ≤ 8 → x ≥ -3.5. So the full solution set is x ∈ [-3.5, 4.5]. The integers in it are -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — that is 8 integers. Answer: (c) 8.

traps: Dropping the absolute-value sign without splitting into cases (or squaring carelessly) introduces extraneous roots — always verify candidate solutions.
Off-by-one when counting integers in a closed interval: [-3.5, 4.5] contains 8 integers (-3 through 4), not 7.
Flipping the inequality sign is required when you multiply/divide by a negative — a very common slip.

subtopics.name: Functions & graphs
subtopics.summary: Composition, inverses, even/odd behaviour, and functional equations, plus reading transformations off a graph. XAT's signature here is the functional-equation question — you are given a relation like f(x) + 2f(1/x) = ... and must find one value by substituting a second, cleverly chosen input and solving the resulting linear system.
formulas: Composition: (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x)); in general f(g(x)) ≠ g(f(x))
Inverse: if y = f(x) then x = f^{-1}(y); graphs of f and f^{-1} are mirror images across y = x
Even function: f(-x) = f(x) (symmetric about y-axis); Odd function: f(-x) = -f(x) (symmetric about origin)
Transformations of y = f(x): f(x - a) shifts right by a, f(x) + b shifts up by b, -f(x) reflects in x-axis, f(-x) reflects in y-axis
Number of times each output is hit = number of intersections of the graph with a horizontal line (horizontal-line test for one-to-one/invertibility)
keyIdeas: For a functional equation relating f(x) and f(1/x) (or f(x) and f(-x), or f(x) and f(1-x)), plug in the 'partner' input to get a second equation, then solve the two as simultaneous linear equations in f(x) and f(partner).
Test even/odd by substituting -x; test periodicity or symmetry by substituting the specific transform the equation suggests.
For composed/nested functions, always work from the innermost bracket outward and keep track of the domain at each step.

example.q:
A function f defined for all x ≠ 0 satisfies f(x) + 2f(1/x) = 3x. What is f(2)?
(a) -2  (b) -1  (c) 0  (d) 1  (e) 2

traps: Assuming f(g(x)) = g(f(x)); composition is not commutative in general.
In functional equations, trying to 'find a formula for f' when you only need one value — substituting the partner input and eliminating is far faster.
Ignoring domain restrictions (here x ≠ 0), which can silently drop or add solutions.

subtopics.name: Progressions & series (AP, GP, HP)
subtopics.summary: Arithmetic, geometric and harmonic progressions, their nth terms and sums, including infinite GPs. XAT leans on progressions more than CAT: expect infinite-GP sums, mixing two conditions (e.g. sum and sum-of-squares), and HP problems that are solved by flipping to an AP.
formulas: AP: nth term a_n = a + (n - 1)d; sum S_n = (n/2)[2a + (n - 1)d] = (n/2)(first + last)
GP: nth term a_n = a·r^{n-1}; sum S_n = a(r^n - 1)/(r - 1) for r ≠ 1
Infinite GP: S_∞ = a/(1 - r), valid only when |r| < 1
HP: the reciprocals of the terms form an AP; the harmonic mean of a and b is 2ab/(a + b)
Means: AM = (a+b)/2 ≥ GM = √(ab) ≥ HM = 2ab/(a+b); also GM^2 = AM·HM
keyIdeas: For an infinite GP, the squares of its terms also form a GP with first term a^2 and ratio r^2, so sum of squares = a^2/(1 - r^2) — pairing this with a/(1 - r) lets you solve for a and r quickly.
Any HP problem: take reciprocals to convert it to an AP, solve there, then reciprocate back.
When a middle term or symmetry is present, write consecutive terms as a - d, a, a + d (AP) or a/r, a, ar (GP) to make the sum/product conditions symmetric and easy.

example.q:
The sum of an infinite geometric progression is 15 and the sum of the squares of its terms is 45. What is the first term of the progression?
(a) 3  (b) 5  (c) 6  (d) 9  (e) 10

traps: Using S_∞ = a/(1 - r) when |r| ≥ 1 — an infinite GP only converges for |r| < 1.
Averaging the terms of an HP as if it were an AP; you must flip to reciprocals first.
Sign/counting slips in AP sums: S_n uses (n - 1)d, not nd, and n must be a positive integer.

subtopics.name: Logarithms, surds & indices
subtopics.summary: Manipulating logarithms (product/quotient/power and change-of-base rules), simplifying surds, and applying exponent laws. XAT tests logs noticeably more than CAT — commonly multi-base expressions (log to bases 2, 4, 16 in one equation) that collapse once you convert everything to a single base.
formulas: log_b(xy) = log_b x + log_b y; log_b(x/y) = log_b x - log_b y; log_b(x^n) = n·log_b x
Change of base: log_b x = (log_c x)/(log_c b); special case log_b x = 1/(log_x b)
log_{b^k} x = (1/k)·log_b x; b^{log_b x} = x; log_b b = 1; log_b 1 = 0
Index laws: a^m·a^n = a^{m+n}; (a^m)^n = a^{mn}; a^{-n} = 1/a^n; a^{1/n} = the nth root of a
Rationalising a surd: multiply by the conjugate, e.g. 1/(√a + √b) = (√a - √b)/(a - b)
keyIdeas: When several bases appear (2, 4, 8, 16 …), rewrite every log to the smallest base: log_{2^k} x = (log_2 x)/k. Substitute t = log_2 x and the equation becomes a simple linear one in t.
A useful sanity move: log_b x = 1/log_x b lets you flip an awkward base into the exponent slot.
For surds, rationalise with the conjugate to clear roots from the denominator; for nested radicals, try to write the inside as a perfect square (√a ± √b)^2.

example.q:
If log_2 x + log_4 x + log_16 x = 7, then x equals
(a) 4  (b) 8  (c) 16  (d) 32  (e) 64
example.solution: Convert every term to base 2. Since log_4 x = (log_2 x)/2 and log_16 x = (log_2 x)/4, let t = log_2 x. The equation becomes t + t/2 + t/4 = 7 → (4t + 2t + t)/4 = 7 → 7t/4 = 7 → t = 4. So log_2 x = 4 → x = 2^4 = 16. Answer: (c) 16.

traps: Writing log(x + y) = log x + log y — false. The product rule applies to log(xy), not log(x + y).
Forgetting the domain: the argument of a log must be positive and the base must be positive and ≠ 1, so discard any solution that violates this.
Mishandling fractional/negative indices, e.g. treating a^{1/2} as a·(1/2) instead of √a.

subtopics.name: Maxima & minima
subtopics.summary: Finding the largest/smallest value of an expression, usually without calculus: completing the square for a quadratic, or applying AM ≥ GM to positive quantities. XAT dresses this up as an algebraic 'minimum value of this expression' question or a geometric optimisation (fixed perimeter/sum → maximise area/product).
formulas: Quadratic f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c: extremum at x = -b/(2a); the extreme value is c - b^2/(4a) = (4ac - b^2)/(4a). It is a minimum if a > 0, a maximum if a < 0.
AM ≥ GM: for positive reals, x + k/x ≥ 2√k, with equality when x = √k
Fixed sum → maximum product when the parts are equal; fixed product → minimum sum when the parts are equal
a·sinθ + b·cosθ ranges over [-√(a^2 + b^2), √(a^2 + b^2)]
Three-sides fencing / open-box type: model area or volume as a quadratic (or use AM ≥ GM) and optimise
keyIdeas: If the expression has the form (variable) + (constant)/(variable) with a positive variable, apply AM ≥ GM to read off the minimum directly — no calculus needed.
For anything that reduces to a quadratic, complete the square (or use x = -b/2a); the sign of the leading coefficient tells you whether it is a max or a min.
Symmetry heuristic: when a quantity is optimised subject to a fixed sum or fixed product, the optimum almost always occurs when the variables are equal.

example.q:
For x > 0, the minimum value of (x^2 + x + 1)/x is
(a) 1  (b) 2  (c) 3  (d) 4  (e) 5
example.solution: Split the fraction: (x^2 + x + 1)/x = x + 1 + 1/x. Since x > 0, by AM ≥ GM, x + 1/x ≥ 2√(x·1/x) = 2, with equality when x = 1. Hence the whole expression is ≥ 2 + 1 = 3, and the minimum value 3 is attained at x = 1. Answer: (c) 3.

traps: Applying AM ≥ GM to terms that are not all positive — the inequality requires non-negative quantities, so check the domain (here x > 0) first.
Reporting only the bound 2 for x + 1/x and forgetting the leftover constant term (+1) in the expression.
Assuming the vertex x = -b/2a always gives a minimum; it gives a maximum when a < 0.

subtopics.name: Triangles
subtopics.summary: The single most-tested geometry area on XAT. Expect right-triangle work, area via Heron/altitude, similar triangles, and inradius/circumradius results — XAT geometry runs harder than CAT, so triangle fundamentals must be automatic.
formulas: Area = (1/2) · base · height = (1/2)·ab·sin C
Heron's: s = (a+b+c)/2, Area = √(s(s−a)(s−b)(s−c))
Area = r·s (r = inradius) = abc/(4R) (R = circumradius)
Pythagoras: a² + b² = c² (right angle opposite hypotenuse c)
Right triangle: circumradius R = hypotenuse/2, inradius r = (a + b − c)/2
Angle bisector theorem: BD/DC = AB/AC (AD bisects ∠A onto BC)
Apollonius (median AD to BC): AB² + AC² = 2(AD² + BD²)
Similar triangles: (area ratio) = (corresponding side ratio)²
30-60-90 sides 1 : √3 : 2; 45-45-90 sides 1 : 1 : √2; exterior angle = sum of two remote interior angles
keyIdeas: Memorize the Pythagorean triples (3-4-5, 5-12-13, 8-15-17, 7-24-25, 9-40-41, 20-21-29) — they let you skip the square roots.
Heron's is a last resort: if there's a right angle or a known triple, use (1/2)·base·height instead.
Area ratio of similar triangles = (side ratio)² — the go-to for midpoint / triangle-inside-triangle problems.
For any cevian or angle bisector, reach first for the angle-bisector theorem or Apollonius/Stewart.

example.q: A right-angled triangle has its two legs of length 9 cm and 12 cm. What is the sum of its inradius and circumradius (in cm)?  (a) 9  (b) 9.5  (c) 10  (d) 10.5  (e) 11
example.solution: Hypotenuse = √(9² + 12²) = √(81 + 144) = √225 = 15. For a right triangle the circumradius is half the hypotenuse: R = 15/2 = 7.5. The inradius is r = (a + b − c)/2 = (9 + 12 − 15)/2 = 6/2 = 3. Sum = 7.5 + 3 = 10.5 cm. Answer: (d) 10.5.

traps: Triangle inequality: any two sides must sum to MORE than the third — XAT plants 'impossible triangle' options.
Circumradius = hyp/2 holds ONLY for right triangles; don't apply it to a general triangle.
The altitude to a side is not itself a side — plug the right lengths into (1/2)·base·height.

subtopics.name: Circles
subtopics.summary: A staple of XAT geometry, tested more deeply than on CAT: tangents, chords, the power-of-a-point relations, inscribed-vs-central angles, and cyclic quadrilaterals. Often bundled with a triangle inside or around the circle.
formulas: Circumference = 2πr, Area = πr²
Arc length = (θ/360)·2πr; Sector area = (θ/360)·πr²
Chord length = 2·√(r² − d²), d = perpendicular distance from centre
Tangent length from external point P = √(D² − r²), D = distance of P from centre; two tangents from a point are equal
Central angle = 2 × inscribed angle on the same arc; angle in a semicircle = 90°; tangent ⊥ radius at contact
Power of a point (two chords through P): PA·PB = PC·PD
Tangent–secant from P: PT² = PA·PB (PB = far intersection)
Cyclic quadrilateral: opposite angles sum to 180°; Ptolemy: AC·BD = AB·CD + AD·BC
keyIdeas: Power of a point unifies chord-chord, secant-secant and tangent-secant — the same product comes from any line through the point.
Drop a perpendicular from the centre to a chord: it bisects the chord and gives a right triangle (r, half-chord, distance d).
'Angle in a semicircle = 90°' converts any diameter into a right-triangle setup.
Tangent ⊥ radius: every tangent-length problem collapses to Pythagoras with the radius.

example.q: From an external point P, a tangent PT of length 12 cm touches a circle. A secant from P cuts the circle at A and B, with A the nearer point and PA = 8 cm. What is the length of chord AB (in cm)?  (a) 8  (b) 9  (c) 10  (d) 12  (e) 16
example.solution: By the tangent–secant power of a point, PT² = PA · PB. So 12² = 8 · PB → 144 = 8·PB → PB = 18 cm. PB is the distance to the far point, so AB = PB − PA = 18 − 8 = 10 cm. Answer: (c) 10.

traps: In PT² = PA·PB, PB is the WHOLE secant (to the far point), not the chord AB — a very common slip.
The central angle is twice, not equal to, the inscribed angle on the same arc.
Chord formula uses the PERPENDICULAR distance from the centre, not any slanted segment.

subtopics.name: Polygons & quadrilaterals
subtopics.summary: Regular-polygon angle and diagonal counts, plus areas of special quadrilaterals (parallelogram, rhombus, trapezium, cyclic quad). XAT likes the angle-relation and 'how many sides' style, sometimes with a quadrilateral embedded in a larger figure.
formulas: Sum of interior angles of an n-gon = (n − 2)·180°; each interior angle of a regular n-gon = (n − 2)·180°/n
Each exterior angle of a regular n-gon = 360°/n; exterior angles of any convex polygon sum to 360°
Number of diagonals = n(n − 3)/2
Parallelogram area = base × height = ab·sin θ; rhombus area = (1/2)·d₁·d₂ = a²·sin θ
Trapezium area = (1/2)(sum of parallel sides)·height
Cyclic quadrilateral area (Brahmagupta): √((s−a)(s−b)(s−c)(s−d)), s = (a+b+c+d)/2
Regular hexagon (side a) area = (3√3/2)·a²; square area from diagonal = d²/2, diagonal = a√2
Rectangle diagonal = √(l² + w²)
keyIdeas: Exterior angles of ANY convex polygon sum to 360° — the fastest route to a 'how many sides' answer.
For regular polygons work with the exterior angle (360°/n); it's cleaner than the interior formula.
Rhombus and kite: use the perpendicular diagonals to split the figure into right triangles.
Brahmagupta's formula gives the MAXIMUM area for a quadrilateral with given sides, attained when it is cyclic.

example.q: In a regular polygon, each interior angle exceeds each exterior angle by 132°. How many sides does the polygon have?  (a) 12  (b) 13  (c) 15  (d) 18  (e) 20
example.solution: At each vertex, interior + exterior = 180° and (given) interior − exterior = 132°. Adding: 2·interior = 312° is not needed — subtracting gives 2·exterior = 180° − 132° = 48°, so exterior = 24°. Number of sides n = 360°/exterior = 360°/24° = 15. (Check: interior = 156°, and 156 − 24 = 132.) Answer: (c) 15.

traps: (n − 2)·180° is the SUM of interior angles, not one angle — divide by n for a single angle.
Area formulas need the perpendicular height, not the slant side.
Diagonal count is n(n − 3)/2 — the −3 removes the vertex itself and its two neighbours; don't forget it.

subtopics.name: Coordinate geometry
subtopics.summary: Distance, section formula, slopes, straight-line equations, distance from a point to a line, and areas from coordinates. XAT typically frames it as lines cutting each other or the axes to form a region whose area or vertex you must find.
formulas: Distance = √((x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)²); Midpoint = ((x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2)
Section formula (internal m:n) = ((mx₂+nx₁)/(m+n), (my₂+ny₁)/(m+n)); Centroid = ((x₁+x₂+x₃)/3, (y₁+y₂+y₃)/3)
Slope = (y₂−y₁)/(x₂−x₁); parallel ⇒ equal slopes; perpendicular ⇒ product of slopes = −1
Line forms: y = mx + c and ax + by + c = 0
Distance from (x₀,y₀) to ax + by + c = 0 is |ax₀ + by₀ + c| / √(a² + b²)
Area of triangle = (1/2)|x₁(y₂−y₃) + x₂(y₃−y₁) + x₃(y₁−y₂)|
Circle: (x − h)² + (y − k)² = r²
keyIdeas: The shoelace/determinant area formula handles any triangle straight from coordinates — no need to hunt for base and height.
Perpendicular ⇒ slopes multiply to −1; parallel ⇒ equal slopes — this settles most 'what shape is it' questions.
To find where two lines meet, solve them simultaneously; for a line with the axes, set x = 0 and y = 0 for the intercepts.
Three points are collinear ⇔ the 'triangle' area formula gives 0 — a handy test.

example.q: Find the area of the triangle formed by the three lines y = x, y = 2x and x + y = 6 (in square units).  (a) 2  (b) 3  (c) 4  (d) 5  (e) 6
example.solution: Vertices are the pairwise intersections. y = x and y = 2x meet at the origin (0, 0). y = x with x + y = 6 gives 2x = 6 → (3, 3). y = 2x with x + y = 6 gives 3x = 6 → (2, 4). Area = (1/2)|0(3 − 4) + 3(4 − 0) + 2(0 − 3)| = (1/2)|0 + 12 − 6| = (1/2)(6) = 3. Answer: (b) 3.

traps: The area formula needs the absolute value — a negative determinant only means clockwise ordering, not negative area.
Section formula order matters: the m:n ratio weights the FAR point (x₂, y₂) by m.
A vertical line has undefined slope (x₂ = x₁) — don't divide by zero.

subtopics.name: Trigonometry
subtopics.summary: An XAT signature that CAT does not test at all — so it is worth dedicated prep for XAT even though it is optional for other exams. XAT keeps it applied: right-triangle ratios, the 0/30/45/60/90 special values and identities, and above all heights-and-distances (angle of elevation/depression) word problems, with the sine/cosine rule appearing occasionally.
formulas: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1; 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ; 1 + cot²θ = cosec²θ
Special values: sin30 = 1/2, sin45 = 1/√2, sin60 = √3/2; tan30 = 1/√3, tan45 = 1, tan60 = √3
sin(A±B) = sinA cosB ± cosA sinB; cos(A±B) = cosA cosB ∓ sinA sinB; tan(A±B) = (tanA ± tanB)/(1 ∓ tanA tanB)
sin2θ = 2 sinθ cosθ; cos2θ = 1 − 2sin²θ = 2cos²θ − 1
Complementary: sin(90° − θ) = cosθ, tan(90° − θ) = cotθ
Sine rule: a/sinA = b/sinB = c/sinC = 2R; Cosine rule: c² = a² + b² − 2ab·cos C
Triangle area = (1/2)·ab·sin C; heights & distances: tan(angle of elevation) = height / horizontal distance
keyIdeas: Learn the 0/30/45/60/90 table cold — most XAT trig plugs these exact values.
Heights & distances: draw the figure, mark the right angle, write tan(angle) = opposite/adjacent for each triangle, then relate the horizontal distances.
When an expression looks messy, convert everything to sin and cos or apply sin²θ + cos²θ = 1.
For identity/simplification questions, transform the more complicated side toward the simpler one.

example.q: From the top of a vertical tower, the angles of depression of two points on the ground, on the same side and in line with the base, are 30° and 60°. The two points are 40 m apart. What is the height of the tower?  (a) 20 m  (b) 20√3 m  (c) 40 m  (d) 40√3 m  (e) 60 m
example.solution: Let the height be h. The angle of depression equals the angle of elevation from each ground point. For the nearer point (steeper 60°): tan60° = h/x → x = h/√3. For the farther point (30°): tan30° = h/(x + 40) → x + 40 = h√3. Subtract: h√3 − h/√3 = 40 → h·(3 − 1)/√3 = 40 → h·(2/√3) = 40 → h = 20√3 m (≈ 34.64 m). Check: near distance = 20, far distance = 60, difference 40. Answer: (b) 20√3 m.

traps: An angle of depression is measured from the horizontal downward; it equals the elevation angle at the ground point (alternate angles) — place it correctly, not inside the triangle at the wrong vertex.
The steeper angle (60°) belongs to the NEARER point; swapping the two angles flips the answer.
Don't confuse the special values: sin30 = 1/2 but sin60 = √3/2, not the reverse.

subtopics.name: Mensuration (2D & 3D solids)
subtopics.summary: Areas of 2D shapes and volumes/surface areas of cube, cuboid, cylinder, cone, sphere, hemisphere, prism and pyramid. XAT favours combination solids, melting-and-recasting (volume-conservation) problems and surface-area comparisons — expect at least one solid-geometry question per paper.
formulas: Cube: volume = a³, total surface = 6a², space diagonal = a√3
Cuboid: volume = lbh, total surface = 2(lb + bh + hl), diagonal = √(l² + b² + h²)
Cylinder: volume = πr²h, curved surface = 2πrh, total surface = 2πr(r + h)
Cone: volume = (1/3)πr²h, slant l = √(r² + h²), curved surface = πrl, total = πr(r + l)
Sphere: volume = (4/3)πr³, surface = 4πr²
Hemisphere: volume = (2/3)πr³, curved surface = 2πr², total surface = 3πr²
Prism: volume = base area × height; Pyramid: volume = (1/3) × base area × height
keyIdeas: Melting/recasting conserves VOLUME — set total volume before = total volume after, and the π's cancel.
For a cone, always compute the slant height l = √(r² + h²) before using curved surface area.
Keep π symbolic until the end; it usually cancels (recasting) or pairs with a friendly radius.
Distinguish curved (lateral) from total surface — check whether the base/top is included.

example.q: A solid metal cylinder of radius 6 cm and height 32 cm is melted and recast into small solid spheres, each of radius 3 cm. How many complete spheres can be made?  (a) 24  (b) 28  (c) 30  (d) 32  (e) 36
example.solution: Volume is conserved. Cylinder volume = πr²h = π·6²·32 = π·36·32 = 1152π cm³. Each sphere = (4/3)πr³ = (4/3)π·3³ = (4/3)π·27 = 36π cm³. Number = 1152π / 36π = 32. Answer: (d) 32.
