traps: Sphere volume is (4/3)πr³ but its surface is 4πr² — sliding the 4/3 into the surface area is the classic error.
Cone volume carries the 1/3 factor; forgetting it triples the answer.
Radius vs diameter: if a diameter is given, halve it before substituting.
'Total surface area' of a cylinder includes the two circular ends (2πr²); curved surface does not.

subtopics.name: HCF & LCM
subtopics.summary: Highest Common Factor (GCD) and Least Common Multiple of integers, and the standard word-problems built on them (bells/lights ringing together, largest divider leaving fixed remainders, smallest number leaving equal remainders). XAT usually embeds HCF/LCM inside a remainder or a fractions setup rather than asking it plainly.
formulas: For two numbers: HCF(a,b) × LCM(a,b) = a × b (holds for exactly two numbers, not three or more)
LCM of fractions = LCM(of numerators) / HCF(of denominators)
HCF of fractions = HCF(of numerators) / LCM(of denominators)
Largest number dividing a, b, c leaving remainders p, q, r respectively = HCF(a−p, b−q, c−r)
Largest number that divides a, b, c leaving the SAME (unknown) remainder = HCF(a−b, b−c, a−c)
Smallest number leaving the same remainder k on division by a, b, c = LCM(a,b,c)·n + k
Smallest number leaving remainders that are each a constant d less than the divisor = LCM(a,b,c) − d
keyIdeas: Prime-factorise both numbers: HCF takes the LOWEST power of each common prime, LCM takes the HIGHEST power of every prime that appears.
For 'largest number that divides X and Y leaving remainders r1, r2', subtract the remainders first, then take HCF of the results — this is XAT's favourite disguise.
For 'they ring/flash together again' problems, the answer is the LCM of the individual intervals; the number of times in T seconds is T/LCM (+1 if you count the start).
Use HCF×LCM = product ONLY for two numbers; for three-number problems fall back to prime factorisation.

example.q: What is the largest number that divides 1657 and 2037, leaving remainders 6 and 5 respectively?  (A) 123  (B) 127  (C) 235  (D) 305  (E) 381
example.solution: Subtract the remainders so the divisor goes in exactly: 1657 − 6 = 1651 and 2037 − 5 = 2032. The required number is HCF(1651, 2032). By Euclid: 2032 − 1651 = 381; 1651 = 4×381 + 127; 381 = 3×127 + 0, so HCF = 127. Check: 1651/127 = 13 and 2032/127 = 16, both exact. Answer: (B) 127.

traps: Forgetting to subtract the remainders before taking the HCF — HCF(1657, 2037) is the wrong quantity.
Using HCF×LCM = product when there are three or more numbers; that identity is only valid for two numbers.

subtopics.name: Divisibility & factors
subtopics.summary: Counting and summing the divisors of a number from its prime factorisation, plus divisibility rules (7, 8, 11, etc.). XAT rarely asks a bare 'is it divisible' question — it asks for the number of factors, even/odd factors, perfect-square factors, or the number of ways to write N as a product of two numbers.
formulas: If N = p1^a1 · p2^a2 · … · pk^ak, then number of divisors d(N) = (a1+1)(a2+1)…(ak+1)
Sum of all divisors = ∏ (pi^(ai+1) − 1)/(pi − 1)
Product of all divisors = N^(d(N)/2)
Number of perfect-square divisors = ∏ ( floor(ai/2) + 1 )
Number of ways to write N as a product of two factors = d(N)/2 (if N is not a perfect square); = (d(N)+1)/2 (if N is a perfect square)
Divisibility by 8: last three digits form a multiple of 8; by 11: (sum of digits in odd places − sum in even places) is a multiple of 11 (including 0)
keyIdeas: Everything starts from the prime factorisation — get N = p^a·q^b·… first, then all counts are just combinatorics on the exponents.
Even divisors of N = total divisors − odd divisors; the odd divisors are the divisors of N with all factors of 2 removed.
Perfect-square divisors need every exponent even, so each prime pi contributes floor(ai/2)+1 independent choices (0, 2, 4, …).
Number of factors is odd if and only if N is a perfect square (divisors pair up except the square root).

example.q: How many divisors of 7200 are perfect squares?  (A) 6  (B) 8  (C) 10  (D) 12  (E) 18
example.solution: Factorise: 7200 = 72 × 100 = (2^3·3^2)(2^2·5^2) = 2^5 · 3^2 · 5^2. A divisor 2^x·3^y·5^z is a perfect square when x, y, z are all even. Choices: x ∈ {0,2,4} → 3 ways; y ∈ {0,2} → 2 ways; z ∈ {0,2} → 2 ways. Total = 3×2×2 = 12. Answer: (D) 12.

traps: Counting x up to 5 (the exponent) instead of only the even values 0, 2, 4 — the divisor must itself be a square.
Confusing 'perfect-square divisors' with 'divisors that are squares of divisors'; only the exponents-even condition matters.

subtopics.name: Remainders & modular arithmetic
subtopics.summary: Finding the remainder of large powers and products on division by a fixed number — the single most tested Number System idea on XAT. Handled with cyclicity, Fermat's / Euler's theorems, and the rule that remainders can be reduced at every step of a sum or product.
formulas: (a + b) mod n = ((a mod n) + (b mod n)) mod n; (a × b) mod n = ((a mod n)(b mod n)) mod n
Fermat's Little Theorem: if p is prime and gcd(a, p) = 1, then a^(p−1) ≡ 1 (mod p)
Euler's theorem: if gcd(a, n) = 1, then a^φ(n) ≡ 1 (mod n), where φ(n) = n·∏(1 − 1/p) over distinct primes p of n
Wilson's theorem: (p − 1)! ≡ −1 (mod p) for any prime p
A negative remainder −r (mod n) is the same as the positive remainder n − r
keyIdeas: Reduce the base modulo n first, then look for a small power that is ≡ 1 or ≡ −1 (mod n); once you hit 1, powers become cyclic.
Fermat/Euler give the cycle length: for a prime modulus p, exponents repeat every (p−1); reduce the exponent modulo that cycle length.
Hunt for a ≡ −1: if a^k ≡ −1 (mod n), then a^(2k) ≡ 1, which often halves the work (e.g. any base ≡ −1 gives remainder 1 for even powers, n−1 for odd).
For a composite modulus, split with the Chinese Remainder Theorem (find remainders mod each prime-power factor, then recombine).

example.q: What is the remainder when 7^100 is divided by 13?  (A) 3  (B) 7  (C) 9  (D) 10  (E) 11

traps: Reducing the exponent by the wrong cycle length — for a prime modulus p the cycle divides (p−1), not p.
Leaving the answer as a negative or oversized residue; always fold it back into the range 0…n−1 (here 9, not −4).

subtopics.name: Base systems & number representation
subtopics.summary: Reading and manipulating numbers written in bases other than 10 (binary, base-5, base-7, etc.), converting between bases, and solving for an unknown base. XAT touches this occasionally, usually as a single conversion or an 'find the base' equation dressed up as a puzzle.
formulas: A number (d_k d_{k−1} … d_1 d_0) in base b equals Σ d_i · b^i (each digit d_i satisfies 0 ≤ d_i < b)
Decimal → base b: divide repeatedly by b; the remainders, read bottom-to-top, are the digits
In base b, appending a 0 multiplies the value by b; the last digit is the value mod b
A k-digit number in base b lies between b^(k−1) and b^k − 1 inclusive
keyIdeas: To evaluate a base-b number, use Horner/place value: multiply the running total by b and add the next digit, left to right.
For 'find the base' problems, turn the base-b string into a polynomial in b, set it equal to the given value, and solve — often it factors neatly.
Sanity-check every digit against the base: a digit like 4 forces base ≥ 5, which quickly rules out options.
Binary tricks: a number that is all 1s in binary (k ones) equals 2^k − 1.

example.q: For which base b does the number 144 (written in base b) equal 100 in base 10?  (A) 6  (B) 7  (C) 8  (D) 9  (E) 10
example.solution: In base b, 144 = 1·b^2 + 4·b + 4 = b^2 + 4b + 4 = (b + 2)^2. Set (b + 2)^2 = 100, so b + 2 = 10, giving b = 8. The digits 1, 4, 4 are all valid in base 8 (each < 8). Check: 1·64 + 4·8 + 4 = 64 + 32 + 4 = 100. Answer: (C) 8.

traps: Reading '144' as the decimal number 144 instead of a base-b string — its value depends entirely on b.
Accepting a base in which some digit is not valid (e.g. digit 4 requires base ≥ 5), or taking the negative root b + 2 = −10.

subtopics.name: Properties of integers (primes, odd/even, last digits)
subtopics.summary: The 'facts about integers' toolkit: primality and the 6k±1 form, parity (odd/even) algebra, and units-digit (last-digit) cyclicity of powers. XAT uses these as fast filters — the last digit of a huge power, or parity to eliminate answer options in seconds.
formulas: Units-digit cycles (period ≤ 4): 2→2,4,8,6 ; 3→3,9,7,1 ; 7→7,9,3,1 ; 8→8,4,2,6 ; 4→4,6 ; 9→9,1 ; while 0,1,5,6 keep their last digit for every power
To get the units digit of a^n, reduce n modulo the cycle length (using the cycle position, treating a multiple of the length as the last term)
Every prime greater than 3 is of the form 6k ± 1 (the converse is not guaranteed)
Sum of the first n odd numbers = n^2; sum of the first n even numbers = n(n+1)
The product of any r consecutive integers is divisible by r! ; parity rules: odd±odd = even, odd×odd = odd, even×(anything) = even
keyIdeas: For the last digit of a power, only the units digit of the base and the exponent-mod-4 matter — discard everything else.
Use parity as an elimination tool: knowing a result must be even (or odd) often kills 3–4 of the 5 options instantly.
To test primality of n, trial-divide only by primes up to √n; and only check numbers of the form 6k±1 beyond 3.
For the last digit of a sum or difference of powers, find each term's units digit separately, then add/subtract and take that result's units digit.

example.q: What is the units digit of 7^95 + 3^58?  (A) 0  (B) 2  (C) 4  (D) 6  (E) 8
example.solution: Units digit of 7^n cycles 7, 9, 3, 1 with period 4. 95 = 4×23 + 3, so it is the 3rd term → 3. Units digit of 3^n cycles 3, 9, 7, 1 with period 4. 58 = 4×14 + 2, so it is the 2nd term → 9. Add the units digits: 3 + 9 = 12, whose units digit is 2. Answer: (B) 2.

traps: Mis-indexing the cycle: when the exponent is an exact multiple of the period, take the LAST term of the cycle (the '1'), not the first.
Adding the full powers or their remainders incorrectly — only the units digits of the two terms should be combined.

subtopics.name: Permutations & combinations
subtopics.summary: Counting arrangements (order matters — permutations) and selections (order doesn't matter — combinations). XAT usually poses 1 word-based counting problem per paper, often framed as committee formation, seating/arrangement with restrictions, or digit/number formation; the trap is almost always deciding whether order matters and handling the 'at least / at most' condition by cases.
formulas: nPr = n! / (n-r)!  — arrangements (order matters) of r out of n distinct objects
nCr = n! / [r!(n-r)!]  — selections (order irrelevant) of r out of n distinct objects
nCr = nC(n-r);  nC0 = nCn = 1;  nCr = (n/r)·(n-1)C(r-1)
Arrangements of n objects with repeats (p alike, q alike, r alike): n! / (p! q! r!)
Circular arrangements of n distinct objects: (n-1)!  (divide by 2 more for necklaces/bangles where flips coincide)
Number of ways to select at least one from n distinct objects: 2^n - 1
keyIdeas: First decide: does order matter? 'Arrange / seat / rank / password' -> permutation; 'choose / committee / handshake / team' -> combination.
For 'at least k' or 'at most k' conditions, either split into cases and add, or compute the complement (Total - unwanted) — pick whichever needs fewer cases.
Handle restrictions first (glue items that must stay together into one block; place restricted items before the free ones).
'Together' = treat the block as one unit then multiply by internal arrangements; 'never together' = Total - Together.

example.q: A committee of 5 members is to be formed from 6 men and 4 women. In how many ways can it be formed if it must contain at least 2 women? (a) 120  (b) 156  (c) 186  (d) 200  (e) 246
example.solution: Complement method is fastest. Total committees of 5 from 10 people = 10C5 = 252. Subtract those with fewer than 2 women. With 0 women (all 5 men) = 6C5 = 6. With exactly 1 woman = 4C1 x 6C4 = 4 x 15 = 60. So at least 2 women = 252 - 6 - 60 = 186. (Check by direct cases: 2W3M = 6x20 = 120, 3W2M = 4x15 = 60, 4W1M = 1x6 = 6; 120+60+6 = 186.) Answer: (c) 186.

traps: Using permutations for a committee/team — a committee has no order, so it is nCr, not nPr.
Forgetting a case in 'at least': 'at least 2 women' includes 2, 3 AND 4 women; the 4-women case (6 ways) is easy to drop.
Double counting when you 'reserve' 2 women first and fill the rest freely — that over-counts committees; always split by exact numbers or use the complement.

subtopics.name: Probability
subtopics.summary: Chance of an event = favourable outcomes / total outcomes, extended with addition, complement, independence and conditional rules. On XAT it appears as a card/ball/dice problem or as a small real-life scenario; the section reward comes from setting up the sample space correctly and knowing when to use 1 - P(none) instead of adding many cases.
formulas: P(E) = (number of favourable outcomes) / (total outcomes),  with 0 <= P(E) <= 1
Addition rule: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)  (subtract nothing if mutually exclusive)
Independent events: P(A and B) = P(A)·P(B)
Conditional: P(A | B) = P(A and B) / P(B)
Complement: P(not A) = 1 - P(A);  P(at least one) = 1 - P(none)
Binomial (n independent trials, success prob p): P(exactly k) = nCk · p^k · (1-p)^(n-k)
keyIdeas: Fix whether draws are with or without replacement, and whether order matters, BEFORE counting — that choice sets your denominator.
For 'both/all same' or 'one of each' with several draws, count with combinations: favourable / total = (nCr of the wanted group) / (nCr of the whole).
'At least one' is almost always faster as 1 - P(none).
Independent means one draw does not change the next (with replacement); dependent means it does (without replacement) — keep the fractions consistent.

example.q: An urn contains 3 red, 4 green and 5 blue balls. Three balls are drawn at random together. What is the probability that the three drawn balls are of three different colours? (a) 3/44  (b) 2/11  (c) 5/22  (d) 3/11  (e) 1/3
example.solution: Total ways to draw 3 from 12 = 12C3 = 220. For three different colours we need one red, one green and one blue: 3C1 x 4C1 x 5C1 = 3 x 4 x 5 = 60 favourable ways. Probability = 60/220 = 3/11. Answer: (d) 3/11.

traps: Multiplying the single-draw probabilities as if with replacement (3/12 x 4/12 x 5/12) — the balls are drawn together, so use combinations without replacement.
Forgetting that 'one of each colour' can arise in any order; the combination count 3C1·4C1·5C1 already handles this, but the sequential fraction method must be multiplied by 3! for the orders.
Reducing the fraction wrongly: 60/220 = 3/11, not 3/22 — divide numerator and denominator both by 20.

subtopics.name: Set theory & Venn diagrams
subtopics.summary: Counting members across overlapping groups using inclusion-exclusion, usually visualised with 2- or 3-circle Venn diagrams. XAT tends to give a survey caselet (people who like/use two or three things) and ask for a specific region — 'exactly one', 'exactly two', 'none', or a max/min value — which rewards region-by-region bookkeeping rather than blind formula use.
formulas: Two sets: n(A∪B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A∩B)
Three sets: n(A∪B∪C) = n(A)+n(B)+n(C) - n(A∩B) - n(B∩C) - n(C∩A) + n(A∩B∩C)
n(only A) = n(A) - n(A∩B) - n(A∩C) + n(A∩B∩C)
Exactly two of the three = [n(A∩B) + n(B∩C) + n(C∩A)] - 3·n(A∩B∩C)
Exactly one of the three = n(A∪B∪C) - (exactly two) - (all three)
None (De Morgan): n(neither) = Total - n(A∪B∪C)
keyIdeas: Fill the Venn diagram from the centre outward: start with n(A∩B∩C), then the exactly-two regions (pairwise intersection minus the centre), then the exactly-one regions.
Remember pairwise intersections (e.g. n(A∩B)) INCLUDE the triple-overlap; subtract the centre to get the 'exactly two' slice.
For max/min questions, push overlaps to the extreme allowed by the totals rather than using a single formula.
'At least one' = the union; 'none' = Total - union; keep these two straight.

example.q: In a survey of 100 people, 40 like tea, 50 like coffee and 35 like milk. 12 like both tea and coffee, 15 like coffee and milk, 10 like tea and milk, and 5 like all three. How many like exactly one of the three drinks? (a) 55  (b) 60  (c) 66  (d) 71  (e) 88
example.solution: Work each 'only' region using n(only A) = n(A) - the two pairwise overlaps + the triple. Only tea = 40 - 12 - 10 + 5 = 23. Only coffee = 50 - 12 - 15 + 5 = 28. Only milk = 35 - 15 - 10 + 5 = 15. Exactly one = 23 + 28 + 15 = 66. (Sanity check: union = 40+50+35 - 12 - 15 - 10 + 5 = 93; exactly two = (12+15+10) - 3x5 = 22; all three = 5; and 66 + 22 + 5 = 93 = union, so none = 100 - 93 = 7.) Answer: (c) 66.

traps: Treating a pairwise intersection as 'exactly two' — n(A∩B) still contains the people who like all three, so subtract the centre before calling it 'only A and B'.
Adding all seven regions and forgetting the 'none' group when the total is given — union need not equal the total.
In max/min problems, applying the inclusion-exclusion identity as if the overlaps were fixed; they are variable, so reason with bounds instead.

subtopics.name: Tables
subtopics.summary: Tabular DI presents raw numbers in rows and columns and rewards fast, accurate arithmetic - the single most common DI form on XAT. XAT tables are deliberately calculation-heavy (multi-year revenues, employee counts, per-unit figures) and often bundle two or three steps (find a value, then a percentage change or a ratio) into one question.
formulas: Percentage change = (New - Old) / Old x 100
Weighted average = sum(weight_i x value_i) / sum(weight_i)
Simple average = (sum of values) / (number of values)
Growth index / ratio = value in later period / value in base period
keyIdeas: Read every row/column header and its UNIT first (thousands vs lakhs vs %, monthly vs annual) - mis-reading a unit is the top table mistake.
Scan the question before crunching: most table cells are decoys, so compute only the ones the question actually needs.
When you combine sub-groups of different sizes, use the WEIGHTED average (weighted by group size), never a plain average of the sub-averages.
With 5 options, round aggressively and eliminate - you rarely need the answer to the last rupee.

example.q:
The table shows the number of employees and the average monthly salary in each of four departments of a firm.
Dept A: 20 employees, avg salary Rs 40,000
Dept B: 30 employees, avg salary Rs 50,000
Dept C: 25 employees, avg salary Rs 60,000
Dept D: 25 employees, avg salary Rs 30,000
What is the average monthly salary across all 100 employees of the firm?
(a) Rs 42,000  (b) Rs 45,000  (c) Rs 45,500  (d) Rs 46,000  (e) Rs 48,000
example.solution: The overall average is the weighted average, using head-counts as weights: (20x40000 + 30x50000 + 25x60000 + 25x30000) / (20+30+25+25) = (800000 + 1500000 + 1500000 + 750000) / 100 = 4,550,000 / 100 = Rs 45,500. A plain mean of the four salary figures, (40000+50000+60000+30000)/4 = Rs 45,000, is wrong because the departments are not equal in size. Answer: (c) Rs 45,500.

traps: Averaging the averages: a simple mean of per-group averages ignores group sizes and gives the wrong overall figure.
Confusing a percentage change with a percentage-POINT change when a column is already expressed in %.
Silently mixing units across rows or columns (Rs crore vs Rs lakh, per-month vs per-year).

subtopics.name: Bar & line graphs
subtopics.summary: Bar and line charts encode magnitudes by height/position and are a XAT DI staple, frequently as a combo chart (bars for an absolute value like revenue, a line for a rate like profit margin %). The exam tests whether you can convert a plotted percentage into an absolute amount and read the correct axis on a dual-axis chart.
formulas: Percentage change = (New - Old) / Old x 100
Absolute value from a rate: Profit = Revenue x Margin%  (value = base x rate)
Net effect of two successive % changes a then b = a + b + ab/100
CAGR over n years = (End value / Start value)^(1/n) - 1
keyIdeas: On a combo (bar + line) chart the two series usually use DIFFERENT axes and units - identify which axis each series reads before touching numbers.
A percentage line gives no rupees on its own; multiply it by the matching bar value to get an absolute amount.
The tallest bar is not automatically the answer - recompute the exact quantity asked for each period and compare.

example.q:
A combo chart shows a company's annual revenue (bars, Rs crore) and its profit margin (line, % of revenue):
2019: Revenue 200, Margin 20%
2020: Revenue 250, Margin 24%
2021: Revenue 300, Margin 22%
2022: Revenue 350, Margin 18%
2023: Revenue 400, Margin 14%
In which year was the absolute profit (in Rs crore) the highest?
(a) 2019  (b) 2020  (c) 2021  (d) 2022  (e) 2023
example.solution: Absolute profit = Revenue x Margin. 2019: 200 x 0.20 = 40; 2020: 250 x 0.24 = 60; 2021: 300 x 0.22 = 66; 2022: 350 x 0.18 = 63; 2023: 400 x 0.14 = 56. The maximum is 66, in 2021. Answer: (c) 2021. Note the built-in trap: revenue peaks in 2023 and the margin peaks in 2020, yet neither of those is the year of highest rupee profit.

traps: Picking the highest bar (largest revenue) or the highest line point (largest margin) instead of the largest PRODUCT (absolute profit).
Reading a value off the wrong y-axis on a dual-axis chart.
Adding two successive percentage growths directly - 20% then 25% is a 50% rise, not 45%.

subtopics.name: Pie charts
subtopics.summary: A pie chart shows how a single total splits into parts, given as percentages or as central angles (100% = 360 degrees). XAT pie questions are calculation-heavy: they attach a rupee total, ask you to move between angle and percentage, or combine two pies with different totals so that equal-looking slices are actually different amounts.
formulas: Central angle of a sector = Percentage share x 3.6 degrees   (since 100% = 360 degrees)
Percentage share = Central angle / 3.6
Sector value = (Percentage share / 100) x Total = (Central angle / 360) x Total
All sectors of one pie sum to 100% (360 degrees)
keyIdeas: Switch between angle and percentage with the 3.6 factor (1% = 3.6 degrees); questions often give a sector only as an angle.
A percentage share is meaningless without the total - fix the total value before comparing across two pies.
Two pies with different totals: identical percentage slices can stand for very different absolute amounts.

example.q:
A pie chart splits a company's total annual cost of Rs 7,200 crore as: Raw material 35%, Labour 20%, Overheads 15%, Marketing 12%, Distribution 10%, Admin 8%. Next year every cost stays the same in rupees except Marketing, which rises by 25%. Marketing's share of the NEW total cost will be closest to:
(a) 13.5%  (b) 14.0%  (c) 14.6%  (d) 15.0%  (e) 15.5%
example.solution: Current Marketing = 12% of 7200 = Rs 864 crore. After a 25% rise it becomes 864 x 1.25 = Rs 1,080 crore. Every other cost is unchanged, so the new total = 7200 - 864 + 1080 = Rs 7,416 crore. New Marketing share = 1080 / 7416 = 0.1456 = about 14.6%. Answer: (c) 14.6%. The trap is answering 12% x 1.25 = 15%, which forgets that the denominator (the total) also grew.

traps: Comparing percentage slices across two pies as if the totals were equal.
Assuming a sector's percentage is unchanged after one component's value changes - the total (denominator) moves too.
Forgetting that the listed sectors must sum to 100% (360 degrees) when back-solving a missing sector.

subtopics.name: Caselet DI (data in text)
subtopics.summary: Caselet DI hides the data inside a paragraph of prose instead of a chart, so the first job is extraction - convert sentences into values, ratios and equations. XAT favours business caselets (revenue/cost flows, survey overlaps, distributions) where a missing constraint or a wrong base quietly wrecks the answer.
formulas: Percentage change = (New - Old) / Old x 100
Value after growth = Old x (1 + growth% / 100)
Operating profit = Revenue - COGS - Operating expenses
Two sets: |A or B| = |A| + |B| - |A and B|; three sets: |A|+|B|+|C| - (pairwise overlaps) + |A and B and C|
keyIdeas: Translate each sentence into a number or an equation as you read; build a small table from the prose before doing arithmetic.
Name the unknowns, then use the stated totals and relationships (percentages, ratios, 'more than', 'of the remaining') to solve step by step.
For 'at least one / both / neither' language, set up a Venn / inclusion-exclusion relation rather than guessing.

example.q:
A startup earned revenue of Rs 50 lakh in 2022; in 2023 revenue grew 40%. Cost of goods sold (COGS) was 60% of revenue in 2022 and 55% of revenue in 2023. Operating expenses were Rs 12 lakh in 2022 and Rs 16 lakh in 2023. Operating profit = Revenue - COGS - Operating expenses. By approximately what percent did operating profit in 2023 exceed that of 2022?
(a) 75%  (b) 85%  (c) 90%  (d) 94%  (e) 100%
example.solution: 2022: Revenue = 50, COGS = 60% of 50 = 30, so operating profit = 50 - 30 - 12 = Rs 8 lakh. 2023: Revenue = 50 x 1.40 = 70, COGS = 55% of 70 = 38.5, so operating profit = 70 - 38.5 - 16 = Rs 15.5 lakh. Percent change = (15.5 - 8) / 8 x 100 = 7.5 / 8 x 100 = 93.75%, about 94%. Answer: (d) 94%.

traps: Missing a constraint buried in a clause ('excluding taxes', 'of the remaining', 'more than').
Applying a percentage to the wrong base (of revenue vs of profit, of the total vs of the leftover).
Solving for an intermediate quantity and forgetting the question actually asked for a change or a ratio.

subtopics.name: Scatter plots & unusual charts
subtopics.summary: XAT is known for non-standard DI - scatter plots, bubble charts, number-matrices and even time-zone tables - where the challenge is decoding what each axis, cell or bubble encodes. In a scatter plot every point is one entity with two attributes (x, y), and questions typically ask for a derived quantity such as a ratio (y/x) rather than a plotted value.
formulas: Ratio at a point (e.g. profit margin, productivity) = y / x = slope of the line from the origin to that point
Positive correlation: y tends to rise as x rises (points trend up to the right); negative: down to the right
Difference metric = y - x (e.g. profit = revenue - cost if both are plotted); read what each axis represents first
keyIdeas: Spend the first minute decoding the chart: what does each axis / cell / bubble-size actually measure? Unusual charts punish guessing.
A rate like margin or efficiency is the SLOPE from the origin to the point (y/x) - steeper means a higher rate, independent of how high the point sits.
In a bubble chart the bubble size is a real third variable; in time-zone / number-matrix DI, first write down the rule that generates each cell.

example.q:
A scatter plot places six firms by Revenue (x, Rs crore) and Profit (y, Rs crore):
A (100, 10), B (150, 30), C (200, 20), D (250, 60), E (300, 45), F (350, 70).
Which firm has the highest profit margin (profit as a percent of revenue)?
(a) B  (b) C  (c) D  (d) E  (e) F
example.solution: Profit margin = y / x. A: 10/100 = 10%; B: 30/150 = 20%; C: 20/200 = 10%; D: 60/250 = 24%; E: 45/300 = 15%; F: 70/350 = 20%. The highest is D at 24%. Answer: (c) D. Note the trap: F has the largest profit (70) and sits highest on the chart, but its margin (20%) is below D's - on a scatter plot the margin is the slope from the origin to the point, not the height.

traps: Equating 'highest point' (largest y) with 'best ratio' (largest y/x) - they can be different points.
Ignoring the third dimension in a bubble chart, where bubble size encodes a real variable.
In time-zone DI, forgetting to apply the zone offset or to handle a date roll-over (previous / next day).

subtopics.name: Data sufficiency
subtopics.summary: Data sufficiency gives a question plus two statements and asks only WHETHER the answer can be determined - not what it is. It appears less often on XAT than on CAT/GMAT/NMAT, but when it does the discipline is the same: test each statement alone, then together, and stop the moment the value is uniquely fixed.
formulas: You are deciding sufficiency, not computing a final value - avoid finishing the arithmetic once uniqueness is clear.
Reference result used below: net of two successive % changes a then b = a + b + ab/100 (e.g. +20% then +25% gives +50%)
keyIdeas: Ask only 'is the value uniquely determined?' - do not fully solve the problem.
Test Statement I alone, then Statement II alone, then both together; never let information from one statement leak into your test of the other alone.
Beware 'looks sufficient': a statement can seem to fix a value yet allow two cases (plus/minus roots, several integers).

example.q:
A stock's price rose in each of two consecutive years. By what percent was the price at the end of Year 2 higher than at the start of Year 1?
Statement I: In Year 1 the price rose by 20%.
Statement II: In Year 2 the price rose by 25%.
Choose:
(a) Statement I alone is sufficient, but II alone is not.
(b) Statement II alone is sufficient, but I alone is not.
(c) Both statements together are sufficient, but neither alone is.
(d) Each statement alone is sufficient.
(e) Statements I and II together are not sufficient.
example.solution: The two-year price factor is (1 + Year-1 rise)(1 + Year-2 rise), so both rises are needed. Statement I gives only Year 1 - insufficient. Statement II gives only Year 2 - insufficient. Together, the factor = 1.20 x 1.25 = 1.50, a unique 50% rise, so both are sufficient and neither alone is. Answer: (c). In data sufficiency you stop once the value is fixed - you need not report the 50%, and you must not add 20 + 25 = 45%.

traps: Actually solving the problem and burning time when only sufficiency is required.
Marking 'both together' when one statement alone already suffices, or vice versa.
Letting a value from Statement I quietly carry into your test of Statement II alone.

sections.name: General Knowledge
sections.note: Part 2, separately timed; NOT counted in the percentile/shortlist (used only at the final XLRI selection stage); no negative marking, so attempt all.

subtopics.name: Business & economy news (deals, M&A, appointments)
subtopics.summary: Corporate and macro-economy headlines of the last 10-12 months: big mergers/acquisitions and stake deals, IPOs and listings, RBI/monetary moves, GDP and inflation figures, and top appointments at banks, regulators and blue-chip companies. This is the single largest and most reliable slice of XAT current-affairs GK because XAT (run by a B-school, XLRI) deliberately tilts toward business and economy, so it rewards a candidate who reads the pink papers. GK is NOT counted in the percentile, so don't over-invest, but there is NO negative marking on the 20-Q section, so attempt all 20 and let business questions be your surest guesses.
keyIdeas: Skim one business daily's front page or a 5-minute newsletter (ET, Mint, Business Standard) and note only the who/what/how-much of each deal, IPO and appointment.
Keep a running one-line list of 'who heads what': RBI Governor, SEBI Chair, chief economic advisor, and CEOs/chairs of top firms that changed hands or leadership.
In the last 4-6 weeks before XAT, do a monthly current-affairs compilation (any coaching PDF or app) and focus its business/economy section hardest.

example.q: Illustrative XAT-style item. In December 2024, who succeeded Shaktikanta Das as the 26th Governor of the Reserve Bank of India? (A) T. Rabi Sankar (B) Sanjay Malhotra (C) Michael Patra (D) Nagaraju Maddirala (E) K. V. Kamath
example.solution: Answer: (B) Sanjay Malhotra, a 1990-batch IAS officer who moved from Revenue Secretary to become RBI Governor on 11 December 2024. Appointments of this kind (RBI/SEBI heads, marquee CEOs) are exactly the business-tilt facts XAT favours. (Specific names are date-sensitive; treat this as a sample of the question TYPE and refresh with a current compilation.)

traps: Confusing the outgoing and incoming office-holder, or the figure with a similar-sounding predecessor.
Mixing up the deal value/stake, or which firm acquired which — read the direction of the acquisition carefully.

subtopics.name: National & international events
subtopics.summary: Major domestic and global developments of the past year: summits and their host cities/outcomes (G20, BRICS, COP, Quad), elections and changes of government, treaties and geopolitical flashpoints, and headline national events. XAT frames many of these with an economic or trade angle (e.g., what a summit decided on trade, currency or supply chains), so read events with a business lens. Like all GK it does not affect your percentile, so cap your time on it, but with no negative marking every one of the 20 questions is worth a shot.
keyIdeas: For every big summit remember four hooks: which city hosted it, who chaired/led, the year, and the one headline outcome.
Track leadership changes in major economies and India's neighbourhood, plus any India-signed agreement or MoU.
Use a monthly compilation's 'national + international' section; events cluster around summits and elections, so those are high-yield to memorise.

example.q: Illustrative XAT-style item. At which G20 Leaders' Summit was the African Union admitted as a permanent member of the G20? (A) Bali, 2022 (B) New Delhi, 2023 (C) Rio de Janeiro, 2024 (D) Osaka, 2019 (E) Hamburg, 2017
example.solution: Answer: (B) New Delhi, 2023. During India's G20 presidency, the New Delhi summit granted the African Union permanent membership, one of the summit's headline outcomes. Summit host + year + one big decision is a classic XAT pairing. (Sample of the question type; verify current-year events from a fresh compilation.)

traps: Swapping the host city or the year across consecutive summits held annually.
Attributing a decision to the wrong summit because the events are close in time.

subtopics.name: Awards, honours & appointments
subtopics.summary: Recent Nobel Prizes and their fields, national civilian honours (Bharat Ratna, Padma awards), major arts/cinema and literary awards (Oscars, Booker, Jnanpith), sports honours, and notable appointments to top constitutional, judicial and institutional posts. XAT leans toward the internationally significant and the business/economics-flavoured ones (e.g., the Economics Nobel), though a couple of national honours usually appear too. GK is outside the percentile so keep prep light, but attempt every item since blanks cost nothing here.
keyIdeas: Learn the year's Nobel laureates by field in one line each (esp. Economics and Peace), plus the latest Bharat Ratna recipients and top Padma Vibhushan names.
Keep a short list of new appointments to marquee posts (CJI, Election Commissioner, UN/IMF/World Bank roles, major-company chairs) alongside the business subtopic.
Awards recur every year, so a single well-made monthly/annual compilation table (award - category - winner) is the most efficient prep.

example.q: Illustrative XAT-style item. The 2024 Nobel Peace Prize was awarded to Nihon Hidankyo, an organisation representing which group? (A) Refugees in conflict zones (B) Survivors of the Hiroshima and Nagasaki atomic bombings (C) Climate activists (D) Journalists in authoritarian states (E) Landmine-clearance volunteers
example.solution: Answer: (B) Survivors (hibakusha) of the Hiroshima and Nagasaki atomic bombings; the Japanese grassroots movement won for its work toward a nuclear-weapon-free world. Nobel Prizes are among the most predictable award questions on XAT. (Sample item; refresh with the latest year's laureates.)

traps: Mixing up the field or the year of a Nobel laureate.
Confusing the tiers of civilian honours (Bharat Ratna vs Padma Vibhushan/Bhushan/Shri).

subtopics.name: Sports
subtopics.summary: Winners and standout performers of recent global and Indian sporting events: cricket (World Cups, IPL), the Olympics/Paralympics and India's medallists, chess, badminton, tennis Grand Slams, football, and any record or first. XAT keeps sports light and factual, favouring headline results (who won, who captained, who set a record) over trivia. It is not scored in the percentile, so spend little time here, but because there is no negative marking, a quick guess on a sports item is free.
keyIdeas: Memorise the latest winners and runners-up of the biggest events only: ICC World Cups, Olympics medal tally highlights, IPL champion, and the reigning world chess champion.
Note India-specific firsts and record-holders (first/youngest to achieve something), which XAT likes to spotlight.
A one-page 'sports current affairs' table from any monthly compilation covers almost everything asked.

example.q: Illustrative XAT-style item. In December 2024, who became the youngest-ever undisputed World Chess Champion? (A) R. Praggnanandhaa (B) Magnus Carlsen (C) Ding Liren (D) D. Gukesh (E) Fabiano Caruana
example.solution: Answer: (D) D. Gukesh (Gukesh Dommaraju), who defeated Ding Liren to win the title at age 18. Headline 'who won / youngest to do X' results are the typical XAT sports format. (Sample item; update winners each cycle.)

traps: Confusing the winner with the runner-up, or the captain with the player-of-the-tournament.
Mixing editions/years of a recurring event held frequently.

subtopics.name: Science, technology & AI
subtopics.summary: Recent developments in space (ISRO missions), the science Nobels, breakthroughs in AI and computing, health/biotech, and major product or platform launches by leading tech companies. Given XAT's business orientation, the tech items often carry a commercial angle (which company launched or acquired what, big AI investments), and AI has become a recurring theme. GK does not enter the percentile, so keep this efficient, but with no negative marking every question deserves an attempt.
keyIdeas: Track ISRO's latest missions and the year's Physics/Chemistry/Medicine Nobels in one line each.
Follow the biggest AI headlines with a business lens: which company built or funded which model or chip, and any landmark launch or acquisition.
A monthly compilation's science-and-tech section, plus a glance at tech business headlines, covers the likely questions.

example.q: Illustrative XAT-style item. The 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded partly for AlphaFold, an AI system that predicts protein structures. Which organisation developed AlphaFold? (A) OpenAI (B) Meta AI (C) Google DeepMind (D) Microsoft Research (E) IBM Research
example.solution: Answer: (C) Google DeepMind; the prize was shared by Demis Hassabis and John Jumper (with David Baker for protein design). This blends science, AI and the corporate 'who built it' angle XAT enjoys. (Sample item; refresh with the latest year's science news.)

traps: Attributing a model, chip or breakthrough to the wrong company.
Confusing which ISRO mission targeted the Moon vs the Sun vs Earth-observation, or their launch years.

subtopics.name: Government schemes & budget
subtopics.summary: The latest Union Budget's headline figures and announcements, key flagship schemes (their sector, target beneficiaries and launch year), tax and policy changes, and major economic-survey highlights. This sits squarely in XAT's business-and-economy comfort zone, so budget allocations, new schemes and who presented the budget are fair game. GK is not part of the percentile, so don't over-prepare, but with zero negative marking, attempt all such items.
keyIdeas: For the most recent Union Budget, remember who presented it, the fiscal-deficit/major-allocation headlines, and 2-3 new schemes or tax tweaks.
Maintain a scheme-name to purpose table (scheme - sector - beneficiary - launch year) for the current flagship programmes.
Budget and schemes are announced on a fixed calendar, so a single up-to-date compilation right after the February budget is the most efficient prep.

example.q: Illustrative XAT-style item. The 'PM Surya Ghar: Muft Bijli Yojana', launched in 2024, provides subsidised access to which of the following for households? (A) Piped cooking gas (B) Rooftop solar power (C) Free health insurance (D) Broadband internet (E) Crop insurance
example.solution: Answer: (B) Rooftop solar power; the scheme subsidises rooftop solar installations to give free/low-cost electricity to about one crore households. Scheme-to-purpose matching like this is a staple XAT format. (Sample item; verify the latest schemes and budget figures from a fresh compilation.)

traps: Matching a scheme to the wrong sector or ministry.
Confusing the launch year, or mixing up allocation figures between two schemes.

subtopics.name: Business & corporate (founders, brands, HQs, taglines)
subtopics.summary: The single largest slice of XAT's static GK (~40% of the 20-Q GK section, since XLRI is a management school and tilts hard toward the corporate world): company founders and current CEOs, brand ownership (which group owns which brand), group/company headquarters, taglines and slogans, logos, mergers/acquisitions and recent business milestones. GK is NOT counted in your percentile, so don't over-invest study hours here — but there is NO negative marking on the 20 GK questions, so attempt every one, and this is the area where a management aspirant can most easily bank easy marks.
keyIdeas: Prioritise Indian corporate GK: founders (Infosys–Narayana Murthy, Wipro–Azim Premji, Reliance–Dhirubhai Ambani), group HQs (Bombay House, Mumbai = Tata Group), and brand ownership (Tata Motors owns Jaguar Land Rover; HUL owns Lakmé/Surf Excel; Titan is a Tata company).

Memorise iconic taglines by drilling matching lists: 'Just Do It' (Nike), 'The Complete Man' (Raymond), 'Neighbour's Envy, Owner's Pride' (Onida), 'Daag Achhe Hain' (Surf Excel), 'Because You're Worth It' (L'Oréal).
Track the last 12–18 months of business news lightly: new CEO/MD appointments, big IPOs and unicorns, and headline M&A deals — XAT mixes a little recent business current affairs into the static pool.
High effort-to-reward: one 20-minute skim of a business-GK compilation before the exam covers most of what's asked; don't build a deep syllabus for it.

example.q:
The long-running corporate tagline 'The Complete Man' is associated with which Indian brand?
(a) Peter England  (b) Raymond  (c) Louis Philippe  (d) Van Heusen  (e) Allen Solly
example.solution: (b) Raymond. The apparel/textiles brand Raymond has used 'The Complete Man' as its signature tagline since the early 1990s. The other four are all men's-wear brands used as distractors, but none carries this slogan.

traps: Confusing the founder with the current CEO (e.g., Infosys was founded by N. R. Narayana Murthy, but the CEO is Salil Parekh) — read whether the question asks 'founder' or 'CEO'.
Assuming an Indian-sounding brand is Indian-owned; many familiar brands sit under foreign parents (and vice versa, e.g., Jaguar Land Rover under Tata Motors).

subtopics.name: Economics & finance (banking, RBI, IMF/WTO/World Bank)
subtopics.summary: The second-biggest static-GK area on XAT, reflecting XLRI's business-and-economy tilt: the RBI (its role, functions, monetary-policy tools like repo rate/CRR/SLR, and its history), the Indian banking system, budget/fiscal basics, and the major international economic bodies — IMF, World Bank, WTO — their headquarters, heads, and mandates. You needn't master economics as a subject: GK is outside the percentile so don't over-invest, but with no negative marking, these are attemptable one-liners worth picking up.
keyIdeas: Lock down RBI static facts: established 1 April 1935 on the Hilton Young Commission's recommendation, nationalised 1949, HQ in Mumbai, and know its monetary tools — repo rate, reverse repo, CRR, SLR, bank rate.

Memorise the 'HQ + head' table for global bodies: IMF and World Bank both HQ'd in Washington D.C.; WTO in Geneva. Heads (verify closer to the exam as they change): IMF MD Kristalina Georgieva, World Bank President Ajay Banga, WTO DG Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala.

Know the current RBI Governor and Finance Minister (Governor Sanjay Malhotra took charge in December 2024; FM Nirmala Sitharaman) — these are stock questions, but confirm the names near your exam date.
Understand a few concept one-liners (fiscal vs. monetary policy, inflation basics, GDP, direct vs. indirect tax) so definition-style questions are free marks.

example.q:
The headquarters of the World Trade Organization (WTO) is located in which city?
(a) Washington D.C.  (b) New York  (c) Geneva  (d) Vienna  (e) Paris
example.solution: (c) Geneva. The WTO is headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland. Note the classic distractor: the IMF and the World Bank are both in Washington D.C. — the pair students most often swap.

traps: Mixing up the three Bretton Woods–era bodies: IMF and World Bank were founded at Bretton Woods (1944) and sit in Washington D.C.; the WTO (1995, successor to GATT) sits in Geneva.
Confusing the year the RBI Act was passed (1934) with the year the RBI actually commenced operations (1 April 1935) — 'established' points to 1935.

subtopics.name: Polity & Constitution
subtopics.summary: Covers the Indian Constitution and governance: key dates, the Preamble, Fundamental Rights and Duties, Directive Principles, the Parts/Schedules/Articles framework, important amendments, and the roles of the President, PM, Parliament and judiciary. It is a smaller slice of XAT's business-leaning GK than corporate/economic material, so keep prep light — but since GK carries no negative marking, learn the handful of high-frequency 'stock' facts and attempt every question.
keyIdeas: Nail the marquee dates and names: Constitution adopted 26 November 1949, came into force 26 January 1950; Drafting Committee chaired by Dr. B. R. Ambedkar; Constituent Assembly first met 9 December 1946.
Map the Parts to their content: Part III = Fundamental Rights, Part IV = Directive Principles, Part IVA (Article 51A) = Fundamental Duties (added by the 42nd Amendment, 1976).

Remember a few landmark amendments (42nd 'Mini-Constitution', 44th, 73rd/74th on local bodies) and stock counts (12 Schedules; borrowings — Fundamental Rights from the US, Directive Principles from Ireland).
This is memorisation-light: a single revision sheet of dates, Parts, and 'borrowed features' covers almost everything XAT asks.

example.q:
Fundamental Duties were added to the Indian Constitution by which Constitutional Amendment?
(a) 24th Amendment  (b) 42nd Amendment  (c) 44th Amendment  (d) 61st Amendment  (e) 73rd Amendment
example.solution: (b) 42nd Amendment (1976). On the Swaran Singh Committee's recommendation, the 42nd Amendment inserted Part IVA (Article 51A) listing the Fundamental Duties. (A later 86th Amendment, 2002, added the duty on education, making the list eleven.)

traps: Swapping the two key dates: the Constitution was adopted on 26 November 1949 but came into force on 26 January 1950 (celebrated as Republic Day).
Confusing Directive Principles (Part IV, non-justiciable) with Fundamental Rights (Part III, enforceable in court).

subtopics.name: History (India & world)
subtopics.summary: Indian freedom-struggle milestones (key movements, sessions, revolts and their years and leaders) plus a lighter dusting of world history (world wars, major revolutions). This is a minor share of XAT's business-tilted GK, so treat it as opportunistic marks rather than a study priority — but with no negative marking, keep the most-repeated dates and leaders ready and attempt whatever appears.
keyIdeas: Focus on the freedom movement's greatest hits: INC founded 1885, Jallianwala Bagh 1919, Non-Cooperation 1920, Dandi/Salt March 1930, Quit India 1942, Independence 15 August 1947.
Attach the right leader to each event (Gandhi–Salt March/Quit India; Bhagat Singh; Subhas Chandra Bose–INA) rather than memorising isolated dates.
For world history, keep only landmark anchors — French Revolution 1789, World War I 1914–18, World War II 1939–45 — since XAT rarely goes deep here.
Very low effort-to-reward relative to business/economy GK: skim a one-page timeline, don't build a history syllabus.

example.q:
The Indian National Congress was founded in 1885 by whom?
(a) Dadabhai Naoroji  (b) W. C. Bonnerjee  (c) A. O. Hume  (d) Surendranath Banerjee  (e) Bal Gangadhar Tilak
example.solution: (c) A. O. Hume (Allan Octavian Hume), a retired British civil servant, founded the Indian National Congress in 1885. A common trap: W. C. Bonnerjee was the INC's first president (who presided over the 1885 Bombay session), not its founder.

traps: Confusing the 'founder' of the INC (A. O. Hume) with its 'first president' (W. C. Bonnerjee).
Mixing up movement years — Jallianwala Bagh (1919), Non-Cooperation (1920–22), Salt Satyagraha (1930), and Quit India (1942) are frequently swapped.

subtopics.name: Geography (India & world)
subtopics.summary: Indian physical/political geography (rivers, mountain ranges, states and capitals, national parks, the Tropic of Cancer) and a little world geography (continents, deserts, longest rivers, tallest peaks). A small slice of XAT's corporate-leaning GK, so keep prep minimal — but since GK has no negative marking, learn the recurring 'trivia' facts and attempt every geography question that appears.
keyIdeas: Remember Indian superlatives and stock facts: longest river Ganga; highest peak entirely in India = Kanchenjunga; the Tropic of Cancer crosses 8 Indian states.
Keep world anchors handy: tallest mountain Mount Everest, largest hot desert Sahara, largest ocean Pacific, longest river (Nile, ~6,650 km, traditionally cited).
Learn state–capital pairings and which rivers/ranges pass through which regions — the most common India-geography question types.
Low priority: a single-page 'geography trivia' sheet is enough; don't spend real study time here relative to business and economics GK.

example.q:
The Tropic of Cancer passes through how many Indian states?
(a) 6  (b) 7  (c) 8  (d) 9  (e) 10
example.solution: (c) 8. The Tropic of Cancer runs through eight Indian states — Gujarat, Rajasthan, Madhya Pradesh, Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, West Bengal, Tripura and Mizoram (west to east).

traps: Calling Mount Everest India's highest peak — Everest is in Nepal; India's highest is Kanchenjunga (Godwin-Austen/K2 lies in the disputed Pakistan-administered region).
Miscounting the Tropic-of-Cancer states (the answer is 8, not the tempting 'nice' number 10).

subtopics.name: Awards, books & authors
subtopics.summary: National and international honours (Bharat Ratna, Padma awards, gallantry awards) and major prizes (Nobel, Booker, Jnanpith, Pulitzer, Oscar), plus 'book–author' matching — including business books and leaders' autobiographies, which fit XAT's corporate flavour. A recurring, easy-to-score area; GK sits outside the percentile so don't over-prepare, but with no negative marking these one-liners are worth banking.
keyIdeas: Fix the award hierarchy: Bharat Ratna = highest civilian award; Param Vir Chakra = highest wartime gallantry award; Jnanpith = highest literary award in India.

Drill famous book–author pairs, tilting toward ones XAT likes: 'Wings of Fire' (A. P. J. Abdul Kalam), 'The Discovery of India' (Nehru), 'The God of Small Things' (Arundhati Roy), plus business memoirs like 'Straight from the Gut' (Jack Welch).
Know recent-year prize winners lightly (latest Nobel Peace/Literature, Booker, Jnanpith) — XAT blends a few current award winners into the static pool; verify these near the exam.
High reward-per-minute: award lists and book–author tables are pure matching — a couple of compiled sheets cover most questions.

example.q:
The novel 'The God of Small Things', which won the Booker Prize in 1997, was written by whom?
(a) Kiran Desai  (b) Salman Rushdie  (c) Arundhati Roy  (d) Aravind Adiga  (e) Jhumpa Lahiri
example.solution: (c) Arundhati Roy. Her debut novel 'The God of Small Things' won the 1997 Booker Prize. The distractors are all real award-winning writers used to tempt you: Rushdie (Booker 1981, 'Midnight's Children'), Kiran Desai (Booker 2006), and Aravind Adiga (Booker 2008).

traps: Attributing a book to the wrong Booker winner when the options are all genuine laureates — anchor each title to its author, not just to 'sounds like an Indian novelist'.
Confusing the highest civilian award (Bharat Ratna) with the highest gallantry award (Param Vir Chakra).

scoring.note: XAT raw scores are LOW and swing with paper difficulty; ~35-40 of 75 is often ~99 percentile. GK is excluded from the percentile. Estimates from coaching analyses (IMS, MBAUniverse, careers360); XLRI publishes no official table.

bm.note: PGDM-BM XAT 2026 official: Male >=94 overall (QA>=85, VALR>=82, DM>=82); Female/3rd >=88; sectional + overall both enforced

hrm.note: PGDM-HRM weights VALR & DM higher, QA lower; Female/3rd 84-87 overall

xlriCutoffs.practical: Aim 96-98+ overall with balanced sections for a safe XLRI BM call.

scoring.selectionNote: XAT percentile -> institute shortlist (sectional + overall cutoffs) -> XLRI stage = WAT/essay + GD + PI; GK score feeds the final selection composite, not the percentile.

strategy.note: Part 1 QA/VA/LRDI overlaps ~80% with CAT. Many aspirants build the base during CAT prep, then pivot to XAT-specific work (Decision Making + full XAT mocks) in the ~5-6 weeks between CAT (late Nov) and XAT (early Jan).

phases.name: Foundation
phases.focus: QA + VA fundamentals (shared with CAT); begin Decision Making caselets; daily dense reading habit

phases.name: Practice
phases.focus: Topic drilling + sectional tests; hammer previous-year papers (esp. DM and QA — XAT repeats patterns)

phases.name: Revision + Mocks
phases.focus: Full-length XAT mocks + deep analysis; DM PYQ mastery; light steady GK; drill the un-attempt penalty timing

mocks.cadence: sectional early, 1-2 full XAT mocks/week in the final month
mocks.rule: For DM, trust official PYQ keys over coaching-mock keys (mock DM keys are subjective); analysis > attempts

sectionTips.valr: RC is ~60% — prioritize it; XAT also tests grammar, vocabulary, fill-in-blanks, analogies, poems and cartoons that CAT dropped, so revive those; everything is MCQ with -0.25, so accuracy first.
sectionTips.dm: Highest-ROI section: no syllabus, no formulas — a fresher can reach 90+ percentile in 1-2 weeks of PYQ practice. Answer as a neutral professional manager: pick the balanced, ethical, stakeholder-inclusive, moderate option; reject extreme, unethical or purely-profit choices; use only what the caselet states. ~10-11 of 21 correct is already strong.
sectionTips.qadi: Toughest, make-or-break section. XAT is heavier on geometry and (unlike CAT) tests trigonometry, plus more pure math (logs/functions/progressions). Concept + PYQ practice beats speed-chasing; ~9-10 correct can fetch ~95 percentile in a hard year.
sectionTips.gk: Not in the percentile and no negative marking — attempt all 20, but do NOT over-invest. Light monthly current-affairs + business headlines is enough; it only matters at the final XLRI selection stage.
sectionTips.unattemptedPenalty: First 8 blanks in Part 1 are free; each blank beyond 8 costs -0.10, while a wrong answer costs -0.25. Track your blank count live: attempt only confident questions in the first pass to protect accuracy, then near the end guess-fill (after eliminating options) to get the blank count down to <=8. The <=8 allowance is one pooled budget across all of Part 1, not per section.
sectionTips.timeStrategy: No sectional timer in Part 1. A common high-scorer split: DM 30-40 min, VA-LR ~50 min, QA-DI ~55-60 min, then GK ~10 min. Start with your strongest section to bank marks; first-pass the sitters, flag the rest, return.

commonMistakes: Bringing personal opinion/emotion into DM and picking the harsh or morally-charged extreme instead of the balanced, stakeholder-inclusive option
Trusting coaching-mock DM answer keys over official PYQ keys, and training on wrong logic
Ignoring the un-attempt penalty — over-skipping past 8 blanks (silent -0.10 bleed) or panic-attempting low-confidence questions (-0.25 bleed)
Treating XAT like CAT — skipping the official XAT mocks (different UI/clock) and under-practicing Decision Making
Over-investing in GK, which doesn't count toward the shortlist percentile
Under-practicing previous-year papers — XAT (especially DM and QA) repeats patterns; the last 7-10 papers are the single best edge
Poor time budgeting — sinking too long into one hard QA/DI set and starving the fast, high-ROI DM section
